body: Expected, `watchMode` changed only when you webpack starts watch (https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/293e677b355da0d5bc4ddfc97d2afec114912711/lib/Compiler.js#L415), if you put code in `apply` `watchMode` will be false, because webpack initializates plugins and nothing more, you need to check it in hook or override from hook.
author: alexander-akait

body:
Thanks @alexander-akait!

I didn't think it was expected, since all I could read in the documentation about it was that the "watch mode" (albeit indeed maybe not the `watchMode` property) brings the `watchRun` and `watchClose` hooks to life...

> The Compiler supports [watching](https://webpack.js.org/api/node/#watching) which monitors the file system and recompiles as files change. When in watch mode, the compiler will emit the additional events such as `watchRun`, `watchClose`, and `invalid`. This is typically used in [development](https://webpack.js.org/guides/development), usually under the hood of tools like `webpack-dev-server`, so that the developer doesn't need to re-compile manually every time. Watch mode can also be entered via the [CLI](https://webpack.js.org/api/cli/#watch-options).

— from https://webpack.js.org/api/compiler-hooks/#watching

I still think it's odd if not inconsistent, but I understand what you're saying, thank you!

Shall I then simply test for `compiler.watchMode || process.env.WEBPACK_SERVE === 'true'` instead of just `compiler.watchMode`?

Thanks again for your swift reply!
author: ccjmne

body:
> Shall I then simply test for compiler.watchMode || process.env.WEBPACK_SERVE === 'true' instead of just compiler.watchMode?

`process.env.WEBPACK_SERVE` is not good idea, but yes, it will work.

Why you need it in `apply`? Maybe better to rewrite on hooks? In most cases it says you should refactor code, rely on it it really not good, you should run logic in hooks, logic should be agnostic to watch or simple build
author: alexander-akait
ccjmne

body: Why not use `afterDone` always? webpack calls this hook for closing too https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/508877c91defb31db9a726f05056e26679b1ac94/lib/Watching.js#L311
author: alexander-akait

body:
The problem with using `afterDone` in "dev" mode is that my Puppeteer instance is shut down in that hook.

I only want to shut it down **once**, when I complete/cancel the watch (in "development" mode), or immediately after the first and only compilation has taken place, in "production" mode.

The `afterDone` hook is called every time a new compilation is triggered and finished, in watch mode... is that right?

EDIT:

Oh, I see my mistake... `process.env.WEBPACK_SERVE` is still set (`true`) even when simply using `webpack --mode production`.
Well, obviously, this environment variable could come from many places anyways, it never was the best solution.

Ideally, I'm really just looking for a simple, consistent way to know whether Webpack is in "watch" mode, either through `webpack --watch` or through `webpack serve`, and I actually can't figure out how to make that happen :(

Unless I'm approaching this problem the wrong way!
Thank you again for your efforts, @alexander-akait :)
author: ccjmne

body:
We have reusable compiler, you can run compiler in watch, when stop and when use it as simple build and again run watch and more and more as you want.

I think you are looking this https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/Compiler.js#L1218

It will be called when `watch` closed and when `just build` finished
author: alexander-akait

body:
Thank you so much, Alexander!!
The `shutdown` hook is indeed what I was looking for!

How come it's not documented anywhere I've looked?
For instance, https://webpack.js.org/api/compiler-hooks/ makes no mention of it.
Is it here to stay?

In any case, it works wonders for me, thanks again for your valuable insight 🙇‍♂️

body: @alexander-akait We just confirmed that without minification and when bundling in dev mode, the bundles are exactly the same. I think we isolated the problem The usage of the arrow function seems to be causing the issue. My guess is that the browser is moving over a massive bit of context and that ends up being a bottleneck on load. Just changing the arrow function to a normal function removed the perf issue. I'm not sure how webpack optimization does this and why, but it appears to be the problem.
author: jljorgenson18

I believe this was the commit where it broke

I think this optimization should be reverted @vankop
author: jljorgenson18

body: @jljorgenson18 You faced with it https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/13125, I recommend to disable arrow functions on your side (https://webpack.js.org/configuration/output/#outputenvironment, set `arrowFunction` to `false`) as a workaround, I think we should report this to Safari team, can you provide version of a browser?
author: alexander-akait

body:
@alexander-akait The problem isn't the overall usage of arrow functions, it's the arrow function that wrap all of the module code. That part is what seems slow and if I had to guess, it's probably due to forwarding the context. This also seems to specific to webpack optimization code and it isn't a problem with the internal webpack code generally.

This happens on ~~both Safari and~~ Chrome, but it probably happens in Firefox as well.

I sincerely doubt we are the only project experiencing this and I can try to find a large open source project tomorrow that is also having this occur. I would expect this to have a negative effect on any large bundle. This was already a tiny optimization related making bundles just the tiniest bit smaller so I think the right course of action is just to revert the change.

That being said, I do think it probably makes sense to report to this to Webkit and Chromium

Edit: We didn't actually confirm it happened on Safari yet

body: @Rush set `output.environment.arrowFunction=false`, see https://webpack.js.org/configuration/output/#outputenvironment . this should help
author: vankop

body:
Could you also test with wrapping the section with `console.time('bootstrap')` & `console.timeEnd('bootstrap')` with dev tools closed, and then inspect the console output afterwards?

Having a debugger attached can slow down specific kind of code considerably because the browser has to keep the context of functions in case you halt the program.

If `console.time('bootstrap')`, `console.timeEnd('bootstrap')` spits out the same time with & without arrow functions, then your customers shouldn't be affected by it.
author: sod

body:
Sorry for the delay. @sod using what you suggested, I confirmed that the issue is still definitely there. Currently on our bundle without minification, bootstrap takes about 160ms **with** arrow functions and about 120ms **without** if you completely wrap the entire bundle.

This is a really simple fix if you just remove arrow functions from the top wrapping UMD function during minification

body:
regarding builded code it should be not `util.xxxx`, code should looks like `global.util.xxxx`. in `speex.js` code global is `this`, but in common js modules `this` is equal to `module.exports`, so `speex.js` doesn't work as bundled version. So you have 2 options:
- build speex to commonjs and use it as module
- or insert speex as script tag and use as external in webpack

author: NaveenMS45

body:
Not sure why so many comments are talking about `react-scripts`, Vue, Vite and Storybook when this is the Webpack repo, not the repo for all those other systems. The issue is a Node issue (https://github.com/nodejs/node/issues/40455) rather than a Webpack issue.

For Webpack itself... I hit the same issue in one of my apps. These were my scripts:
```
    "build-dev": "cross-env NODE_ENV=development webpack --config webpack/dev.js",
    "build-prod": "cross-env NODE_ENV=production webpack --config webpack/prod.js",
    "watch": "cross-env NODE_ENV=development webpack-dev-server --progress --color --port 3004 --config webpack/dev.js"
```

I just added `NODE_OPTIONS=--openssl-legacy-provider` to the `cross-env` call to fix it:
```
    "build-dev": "cross-env NODE_ENV=development NODE_OPTIONS=--openssl-legacy-provider webpack --config webpack/dev.js",
    "build-prod": "cross-env NODE_ENV=production NODE_OPTIONS=--openssl-legacy-provider webpack --config webpack/prod.js",
    "watch": "cross-env NODE_ENV=development NODE_OPTIONS=--openssl-legacy-provider webpack-dev-server --progress --color --port 3004 --config webpack/dev.js"
```

author: alexander-akait

body:
Is my understanding correct that our users would then have to install a webpack plugin to be able to run our custom behavior? That is not particularly useful - for our use-case, there is already a Babel plugin we point our users to, but we want to eliminate having all our downstream users having to extensively configure this, especially when the info we need is available (indirectly)  from the browserlist.

It would be interesting to allow a browserlist query string as the condition, and iff all browsers a user must support pass the query then use that export. This would mean copying/hard-coding a lengthy condition into the package.json for Temporal, but that seems fine to me.

body:
It turned out that this happens when `import.meta.webpackContext` is used inside `html-webpack-plugin` template module.
```js
    new HtmlWebpackPlugin({
      filename: "index.html",
      template: "template.js",
    }),
```

and tempalte.js somethingn like (just for the sake of example):

```
import.meta.webpackContexts()

export default ""
```


It throws the error: `{}.webpackContexts is not a function`

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/736697/172479749-d77f05f4-1697-4709-9aba-5df554972577.png)

Any ideas what is the reason and how this can be fixed?
author: wclr

body: you are using `import.meta.webpackContexts` but should use `import.meta.webpackContext` ( without `s` in the ending )

Exepcted, you replace `process` on `process/browser`, so you need to install it `process`, anyway I recommend avoid it:
1. you should avoid using node.js modules in browser, axious has check on `process` and do good job (error's message says it)
2. if you want to bundle for Node.js you should use `target: "node"`

```
axios tried to access process. While this module is usually interpreted as a Node builtin, your resolver is running inside a non-Node resolution context where such builtins are ignored. Since process isn't otherwise declared in axios's dependencies, this makes the require call ambiguous and unsound.
        Required package: process
```

This error is from yarn and cleary says that you need to install `process`

Shorty - you can't use Node.js module in a browser

author: alexander-akait

body:
The problem is effectively sorted, it used to exports modules and not we have a closure. The side issue, not related to this, is that because I use a `require` as per your solution, the first test passes, but when I generate a second bundle and require it again, node has the old (same file name) in the cache, so it does not update it. 
Interestingly, deleting node module cache seems not an easy and effective solution (as opposed to what the logic would suggest). I am going to use `[contenthash]` or another identifier in the module name which should in theory solve the problem.

body:
I was trying to replicate this issue and noticed as per the documentation, we are multi-part entries are used in this case - https://github.com/webpack/webpack/tree/main/examples/multi-part-library

So this seems like a intentional design choice. Don't you think?

Workarounds
1. Barrel file
2. Add the following to `index.html`
```
        <script src="dist/index.js"></script>
        <script src="dist/scriptB.js"></script>
        <script src="dist/scriptC.js"></script>
```

@TGRHavoc
author: whoami-amrit

body:
> I was trying to replicate this issue and noticed as per the documentation, we are multi-part entries are used in this case - https://github.com/webpack/webpack/tree/main/examples/multi-part-library
> 
> So this seems like a intentional design choice. Don't you think?
> 
> Workarounds
> 1. Barrel file
> 2. Add the following to `index.html`
> ```
>         <script src="dist/index.js"></script>
>         <script src="dist/scriptB.js"></script>
>         <script src="dist/scriptC.js"></script>
> ```
> 
> @TGRHavoc 
> 

Sorry, my memory is a little fuzzy on this issue (I'm trying to use the discussion thread to re-jog my memory https://github.com/orgs/webpack/discussions/15929).
I think the issue was around exposing the exported functions to the HTML from multiple files. As far as I could find out, exporting everything to a global library _should_ have done exactly this but it wouldn't. 
Because I was trying to modernise a very large legacy system, adding additional scripts to every page wasn't feasible and neither was barrel files. In the end, I didn't touch the older stuff and only modernised newer functionality because of this friction with webpack (I originally decided to use webpack because I thought it had this functionality and would allow me to move forward whilst keeping the system functioning). 

Basically, if webpack exposed all exported functions for a named global library, I would expect it to also expose all exported functions for a non-named library. The fact that _only_ the last entry gets exposed does feel like a bug and not intented (maybe it should be all or none?)

Please look at this firstly:
```
npm ERR! While resolving: react-intl@3.12.1
npm ERR! Found: react@17.0.2
npm ERR! node_modules/react
npm ERR!   react@"^17.0.2" from the root project
npm ERR!   peer react@"17.0.2" from react-dom@17.0.2
npm ERR!   node_modules/react-dom
npm ERR!     react-dom@"^17.0.2" from the root project
```

Anyway, I don't see problems here, you don't need to use `weak` here

> 'weak': Tries to load the module if the module function has already been loaded in some other way (e.g. another chunk imported it or a script containing the module was loaded). A Promise is still returned, but only successfully resolves if the chunks are already on the client. If the module is not available, the Promise is rejected. A network request will never be performed. **This is useful for universal rendering when required chunks are always manually served in initial requests (embedded within the page), but not in cases where app navigation will trigger an import not initially served.**

Please read this https://indepth.dev/posts/1483/demistifying-webpacks-import-function-using-dynamic-arguments, here good explain why it happens

author: CTaylorEason

body:
Found the answer! Here is how you update your webpack-cli for anyone else who was curious. 

First, check your version of webpack by running this command in the terminal of your IDE: webpack --version. You should see a list that appears like this: 
webpack: 5.71.0
webpack-cli: 4.10.0     
webpack-dev-server 4.8.0

If your webpack-cli reads anything below 4.10.0, then you need to update it to the latest version for npm to run properly on your localhost with JHipster. Here's how to update it. 

Run this command in the terminal of your IDE: npm install webpack-cli@4.10.0

[Right now, the latest version is 4.10.0, but for the future updates when you run this command, change the version at the end of the command line to updated to that current version: npm install webpack-cli@(INSERT NEW VERSION DIGITS HERE).

Then re-run webpack --version, and see if your webpack has updated to the version that you entered above. 

**********
EXTRA NOTES: 

Why did you have this error? According to webpack.js.org: "Note that this is not a recommended practice. Installing globally locks you down to a specific version of webpack and could fail in projects that use a different version."

So, if you ran a command like this: npm install --global webpack, then there is a good chance that you were locked down to a specific version. This might have been the error. That's what I believe happened to me anyway. I ran npm install -g, or something of that nature. 

Sources for your journey and research that relate to the material above: 
1) https://webpack.js.org/guides/installation/
2) https://stackabuse.com/npm-install-specific-version-of-a-package/

*********

Hope this helps! Best of blessings to ya!

body:
this will not work with esm, since `import x from "x"` or `import("x")` works the same as `require("x")` when there is no `x` => this directives should throw error.
__________
Problem is that by spec all `import x from "x"` executes before module, so you can't do `try {} catch {}`, webpack trying to make esm execution according to spec ( this is expected, since result should be the same if you try to execute code in esm environment, e.g. Node.js ), so I don't see how this could be implemented by webpack..

author: vankop

body:
If everything that can lead to unexpected behavior would be an error, then all our warnings would need to be errors.

For these comment parsing, it can happen that we try to parse a normal comment like `/* webpack allows to add comments here */`, where we definitely don't want to error the build.

So these problems are warnings, because we are not 100% sure that this is an real problem and you can ignore warnings.
Errors are cases where we are really sure that it's wrong.
author: sokra

body:
> If everything that can lead to unexpected behavior would be an error, then all our warnings would need to be errors.

Agreed, however given the increased likelihood of webpack magic comments in general to fail the build, I'd be interested in seeing an option to opt-in to an error on "invalid" magic comments.

If that's undesirable, a couple of alternative suggestions:
1. An option to treat all warnings as errors, effectively failing a build if there's any warning
2. An option to specify the maximum number of warnings (similar to eslint's `--max-warnings` option). If we desire a build with no warnings we can then set it to zero.
3. A more narrowly scoped warning on the webpack magic comment that detects just this use-case where an invalid value was passed to the `webpackChunkName`. Would allow us to catch this error case without interfering with the other ways comeone might add a `/* webpack allows to add comments here */`

What do you think?

body:
This configuration:

``` js
shared: {
  uuid: "^8.3.2",
  // or the equivalent:
  uuid: { requiredVersion: "^8.3.2" },
}
```

will force the version to be `^8.3.2`.
But it's not recommended to do that. You should prefer to let webpack automatically infer the requiredVersion from the nearest package.json (from import location).

``` js
shared: ["uuid"],
// or the equivalent:
shared: {
  uuid: {},
}
```

---

Note you can also mix array and object:

``` js
shared: [
  "lib",
  "uuid",
  {
    react: { singleton: true },
    'react-dom': { singleton: true },
  }
],
```

or use that:

``` js
shared: [
  lib: {},
  uuid: {},
  react: { singleton: true },
  'react-dom': { singleton: true },
},
```

body:
> @DanielRuf We are using regexps in `include`/`exclude`, so we can't validate your path

`include` supports:
* regex
* string
* string array (array of conditions)
* function
...

The issue covers the string / string array method, not regex. Maybe that was a bit unclear from my side (`path.resolve()` generates a string).

https://webpack.js.org/configuration/module/#ruleinclude
https://webpack.js.org/configuration/module/#condition
author: DanielRuf
alexander-akait

body:
> @DanielRuf Why you can't validate paths on your side?

In general webpack config files can have hundreds of lines, many loaders and so on. In our case only a person with Node experience would have spotted this but other developers who try to make these changes get no feedback what is wrong.

> also it reduce build time.

This is why I would make this optional / opt-in via setting - and some "strict" webpack mode.

Please keep beginners, juniors, unexperienced developers in mind - we could improve the DX of webpack with such simple steps.

@eng-dibo when you write:

```
let fileUrl = new URL("file.js",import.meta.url)
```

You create asset module (i.e. webpack copy this file). But as you written above you already have files, so you need to disable it:
1. You can use

```
let filePath = "file.js";
let fileUrl = new URL(filePath,import.meta.url)
```

So it will be not statically analizable, but note - `import.meta.url` will be convert to `file://` (look at generated `webpack.js`, for better debug just disable `devtool: false`)

2. (optional) Also if want to use ESM modules, you need to enable it for webpack and disable bundling `import.meta.url` using configuration - https://webpack.js.org/configuration/module/#moduleparserjavascriptimportmeta, you can special it only for some files using `test`, but looks angular-cli register own loader for typescript files so you need to look at docs how change options for it

i.e.

```
module: {
    parser: {
      javascript: {
        importMeta: false,
      },
    },
  },
```

For `import(...)` you have valid solution, i.e.:

```
import(/* webpackIgnore: true */ fileUrl.toString())
```

webpack keeps `import(...)` as is (looks like you want it)

```
Dynamic import's specifier must be of type 'string', but here has type 'URL'.ts(7036)
```

This error from `typescript` (not webpack), very strange that ts doesn't support `new URL(...)` as argument, but using `.toString()` is good solution here

I tried your reproducible test repo and all works fine:
```
akait@akait-notebook:~/IdeaProjects/dibo$ npm run start:webpack

> ngx-cms@1.1.0 start:webpack
> webpack && node dist/webpack && shx cp src/file.js dist/file.js

asset webpack.js 3.82 KiB [compared for emit] (name: webpack)
runtime modules 937 bytes 4 modules
built modules 334 bytes [built]
  ./src/index.ts 292 bytes [built] [code generated]
  external "node:path" 42 bytes [built] [code generated]
webpack 5.73.0 compiled successfully in 94 ms
file:///home/akait/IdeaProjects/dibo/src/file.js
=====>OK
```

My code:
```
import { resolve } from "node:path";

let filePath = "file.js";
let fileUrl = new URL(filePath, import.meta.url)

console.log(fileUrl.toString())

import(/* webpackIgnore: true */ fileUrl.toString())
  .then(result => console.log(result.default))
  .catch(error => console.error({ error }));

```
author: alexander-akait

body:
In the google bugreport there are reproductions of this, but for me it happens due to an imported component that's compiled with core-js in its sourcemap. Having core-js imported by webpack+babel too - causes duplicate urls. Each url after those in the sourcemap - is treated in an index offset and therefore the breakpoints are not even in the correct file.
Happens in Chrome 103 too, as they have not fixed it yet.

Removing babel solves this for me, but that may not be the case for others. Anyway, a fix [has landed](https://chromium-review.googlesource.com/c/devtools/devtools-frontend/+/3720705/3/front_end/core/sdk/SourceMap.ts) already.
And in the meanwhile, my workaround is to download [Chromium 101](https://chromium.cypress.io/win64/stable/101.0.4951.67) for debugging.

body:
Okay, I done investigation on the latest version of webpack and sass-loader (and other CSS like loaders), shorty:
- `errorStack` allows to enable and disable stack, so it works correctly
- `errorDetails` show details from error in some cases it not looks good, because it duplicate an error message
- `hideStack` works fine and webpack hide stack

The main problem some tools (for example node-sass, maybe due bindling) put stack inside message, so because of `errorDetails` enabled by default you see the stack, but this stack just in error message, so I don't think we can solve it on webpack level...

Anyway I see this problem:
- when `hideStack: true` and `errorDetails: true`, webpack duplicate error mesage, because when we try to print error we print stack + message and here we have duplication
- we should avoid using `this.name` in  `${this.name} : ${error.message}`, because webpack already prints it

Please re-open and try to reproduce it.

body: Because now webpack sees it is different dependecies due different paths (i.e. imports in `import/require`) and you need to modify options in `splitChunks` (because `vendor` by default only for `node_modules`)

body:
I must have missed the notification for this previously @sokra. So to confirm:

Any modules within the `chunks` or `assets` properties of those entries listed via `entrypoints` are expected to be "initial"?

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2090758/197561571-1850c1d9-e1bc-483f-8820-8eaf085a37ab.png)

In that case, these are assumed to be "initial" chunks, while all other children are not "initial":
- "runtime"
- "first"

This is a lot of cross-referencing to do as a consumer. The root of my request here is to make the "initial" property within the list of `chunks` accurate or replace it with a more robust system. We should not need to reference the `chunks` or `assets` properties within each entry point.

hi @ScriptedAlchemy,

thank you for the quick response.

i implemented your suggestion above but seems like it doesn't really solves the issue.
in my case, the issue is when it fails to load a chunk, not the remoteEntry.js.

1. browser loads main.js
2. browser loads all remoteEntry.js files
3. user is working with remote-app-A successfully
4. meanwhile, remote-app-B artifacts has been changed due to a deployment
5. user goes to remote-app-B page
6. browser fails to load remote-app-B chucks

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/5717665/182848041-e984dfbf-d410-4346-94c2-2b733021981c.png)
author: talsi

body:
This is a general problem with any code split application. Not federation specific. 

Keep the previous deploy accessible on your cdn. Like all my assets persist in Akamai for 7 days. Because any user who uses dynamic import. Regardless of Federation, still need to be able to access chunks that are listed on the webpack runtime that was loaded. 

If you don't want to use a cdn to persist then try catch the import and reload the page. No way around it in any application that relies on loading more than 1 file. 

Also this wouldn't be possible given the remote already initialized into webpack, you could reload the remote container but this will cause problems for any already loaded chunks whose references are copied into existing module graph. It could case state tearing. 

There's workarounds, like in the promise new promise. You could resolve custom get and init proxies. If original.get, throws a rejection, you could inject the remote again, initialize it, and call get again with original request

body:
I found it was not caused by `terser` but by `tsc`.

```ts
$ cat ./index.ts
class SomeClass { 
    name = "this is name" 
}
console.log(SomeClass.name);
```

```console
$ npx ts-node ./index.ts
SomeClass
```

This is caused by the existance of `Function.name` in JavaScript.

- [Impossible to define static 'length' function on class · Issue #442 · microsoft/TypeScript](https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/issues/442)

It is not a webpack issue, so close it. thanks.

body: problem in `publicPath`, try to use `publicPath: auto`
author: vankop

body:
> Current behavior happens as document.currentScript.src does not work in case the code is being called in the context of a callback. Amd require, executes the code as callback. Due to this auto-compute logic of publicPath falls back to looking at the src property of the last loaded script. This is highly error-prone.

you have specific logic.. please define `publicPath` in config
author: vankop

body:
Hi,

This is not my specific logic. This is how AMD has always worked ('require' function invoking the factory method registered as callback using 'define').

Besides defining publicPath in config is not an option if the AMD library bundled using WebPack is to be released on Internet. At the level of library the required publicPath can't be known beforehand as it will depend on the user of the library.
author: eramitmittal

body:
Sorry, didn't see it has been marked closed....
Is it right to mark an issue closed without waiting for a confirmation from the reporter? 

Below issue raised separately, is in a way related. Using the last loaded script to determine publicPath assumes too much about how code could be loading.
https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/16041

Even this issue will get resolved if chunk name is used to locate the relevant script tag instead of choosing the last loaded script.
author: eramitmittal

body:
> Besides defining publicPath in config is not an option

not sure what you trying to achieve.. `publicPath` should be used only for applications, not for libraries..

> This is not my specific logic. This is how AMD has always worked

In this case there is no way to determine `publicPath` in `"auto"` mode, so you should define it youself..
author: vankop

body:
> not sure what you trying to achieve.. publicPath should be used only for applications, not for libraries..

Well if a complex enough code (for e.g. monaco-editor) is bundled as a library using webpack and library target is 'amd', wouldn't the webpack output need public path to load chunks or other assets?
Look at the gist I have included in the bug report. The gist is a working example of a library packaged using webpack requiring publicPath to load a chunk. Gist clearly shows the issue.

>In this case there is no way to determine publicPath in "auto" mode, so you should define it youself...

But, there is a way. Locating for the script tag whose url actually contains the name of the current JS file instead of assuming that the last loaded script is the required one. It is not clear why you think it is not a solution.
author: eramitmittal

body:
> It is not clear why you think it is not a solution.

because chunk content hash is based on data inside of this chunk. therefore, you getting circular dependency => in chunk code you want to have something like `this-chunk.hash.js` and same hash in the naming of this chunk...
author: vankop

body:
Understood.

Am I right in thinking that the variable '__webpack_require__.p' (i.e. RuntimeGlobals.publicPath) ever ends up in the main module as defined by "output.filename" option in the config file. The chunks actually never define this variable.

If this is true then, AutoPublicPathRuntimeModule will only ever impact the main file (output.filename) which is not hashed!!!
AutoPublicPathRuntimeModule already determines the chunkName. 
Just that it does not use it to determine the src of the script tag loading the main file.

body:
I will share my insights after a little more investigating:
* When I add `swr` to the shared dependency, it treats it as `commonjs` only when it resolves from the `swr/infinite` context. For example, it resolves correctly when I import it in the index file, and the context is `src/index.js`.
* The reason adding a slash suffix works is because `swr` is not shared anymore (only `swr/infinite` is shared) therefore, it is treated as `mjs` from any context.

I am not sure where [`createConsumeSharedModule`](https://github.com/tzachbon/webpack/blob/00f4a82903d99502ffa1eee5c0bfd2fa405d04b8/lib/sharing/ConsumeSharedPlugin.js#L140) makes it behave like that (hopefully you (@ScriptedAlchemy) will tell me) or the issue is rooted in Enhance resolver.

body: I think the issue is in that file since webpack resolves correctly. It must be the "result" here which isn't resolved to the right dependency

body:
Another small breakthrough for me.
When I added to the [webpack `.mjs` default](https://github.com/tzachbon/webpack/blob/9fcaa243573005d6fdece9a3f8d89a0e8b399613/lib/config/defaults.js#L547) the following config it works:
```
{
	test: /\.mjs$/i,
	descriptionData: {
		type: "module"
	},
	...esm
},
```
The problem is when the consumer extension is `.mjs` too, it breaks for the following reason - 
It calls `swr__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__` and not `swr__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0___default`.

I'm not sure where and when the export type should be "dynamic" or "default-with-named", but it looks like when the `descriptionData.type` is `module` it becomes "dynamic" and uses some kind of `esModuleInterop`.

body: I've been able to figure out that the issue is in the detection of exportsType of a shared module when passing through from another shared module, it comes back as `default-with-named` instead of `dynamic`, which then means that `interopDefaultAccessUsed` never gets set to true, and `compatGetDefaultExport` never gets added to the build. What I can't figure out is _why_ it breaks only when coming in through another shared module. If imported as a first-level shared module, this doesn't happen, the file is correctly typed as dynamic.

author: alexander-akait

body:
This has been solved in v2 as far as I know. The problem is consume share module/plugin. You need to copy over the module attributes from the fallback module. Things like buildMeta 

You can check our version and copy the patch back. Codex discovered the problem, and left a comment on a git issue, and a user sent pr. This also will solve problems one will encounter with something like rsc or next.js flight loaders. In this case the loaders construct and reference manifests based on a modules buildMeta. Consume share module is supposed to act as a dynamic proxy module, it does at runtime- but during compile time, the ConsumeShareModule doesn't faithfully pass through the underlaying modules attributes. So other plugins rely on defaults because they've lost information about how the the original dependency request is constructed.
author: ScriptedAlchemy

body:
I've created a fix for this issue in PR #20054 that resolves the `ConsumeSharedModule` buildMeta/buildInfo inheritance problem.

The fix adds a `finishModules` hook in `ConsumeSharedPlugin` that copies the metadata from fallback modules to their corresponding `ConsumeSharedModule` instances after export analysis is complete.

I've verified the fix works with both reproduction cases mentioned in this thread. The original SWR example with `swr/infinite` `.mjs` imports now works correctly. For @barabaiiika's axios reproduction (https://github.com/barabaiiika/missing_default_export_example), the `Uncaught (in promise) TypeError: axios__WEBPACK_IMPORTED_MODULE_0__.create is not a function` error is now resolved.

The solution is based on the fix already implemented in Module Federation v2 (module-federation/core#4063) and follows webpack's established `DelegatedModule` pattern for metadata inheritance.

PR: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/20054
author: tzachbon

body: I was testing https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/20054 and found that we might want to consider a case where we load a module from remote and the fix is not enough for that. I created another repro to compare this case along with other known cases: https://github.com/naruaway-sandbox/webpack-module-federation-esm-repro And with the PR, I confirmed that "federation-local" is fixed but the "federation-remote" case is not fixed by it. The following is the result of the repro with the PR: Is this considered as a separate issue? I think we should inject default interop for all the import regardless of `javascript/esm` / `javascript/auto` to make the behavior the same across all the variants in the repro
author: naruaway

body: Hi, I found that v2 also has this issue (https://github.com/module-federation/core/issues/4238). On federation-remote, v2 also fails to address `.mjs` file. I created a [PR](https://github.com/module-federation/core/pull/4239) in v2 to address this issue. If that approach is approved, I'll create a PR for this webpack issue's federation-remote case as well.

body:
@hardfist I will add testcases to ensure it, but based on code it should work, let's look at our code, we try to use `link.sheet.cssRules` and if nothing we try to use `getComputedStyle` - https://developer.mozilla.org/ru/docs/Web/API/Window/getComputedStyle, but yeah it can be a problem for `WebView Android` v4.3, on theory we can try to use `el.currentStyle` and for ie8, there are two solutions
- recommend to polyfill it
- try to find another workaround, for very old browsers

But I think it is fine with `recommend to polyfill it` if workaround will be big or have a bad perf
author: alexander-akait

body:
I did some research and fixes (I think we will fix it in multiple PRs, the first will be soon):
- `getComputedStyle` works fine since IE `9` and on old androids https://caniuse.com/?search=getComputedStyle, I think we don't need to support IE8 :smile: 
- CSS custom variables has some problems  - https://caniuse.com/css-variables, for IE and old android

So for the last we can:
- Try to parse all links using `cssText`/`currentScript` and parse text on CSS variables using regexp, it will be slow
- Use `ajax` and using regexp try to extract modules - will be very slow, I don't like it
- Move some logic on HTML level, i.e. developer put `<link rel="stylesheet" data-webpack="webpack:chunk-main" href="./main.css" data-webpack-modules="\.\/src\/foo\.css,\.\/src\/style\.css;">` (it can be done/automated using a plugin), webpack in runtime try to use `getComputedStyle`, if nothing was found we will try to get this `meta` tag and get the chunk name. But here is an limitation - it will work only for initial chunks... But we can use `meta` for async chunks. But it can bloat the source code of HTML page, especial for very big applications.

Our main problem is that we need to understand where and how to store modules which concated into chunk. 

I am now downloading images of old systems to try different approaches. Maybe I can find some other approaches and solutions.
author: alexander-akait

body: Technically, of course, we can try to store all modules (for initial and async) inside JS runtime chunk, this will increase the size of runtime chunk. On the other hand, we can only do this when your don't have `css variables` support in your browserslist and run it as fallback if can't get value using `getComputedStyle`

author: matzeeable

body:
Because you have `import { myFunc } from '../../package-b/index.js';`, webpack skips some optimizations for development mode for perf reasons (otherwise we should analizate more things, i.e. which dependeices are lazy and which are initial and decide to move some modules to own chunks and recalculate graph), shortly - you should not rely on a chunk creation mechanism, because there are a lot of optimizations and chunks can be merged or splitted in future webpack versions, anyway if you really want to force a chunk creation you can do:
1. Move it to own entry and use `dependOn` - https://webpack.js.org/concepts/entry-points/#entrydescription-object
2. Use magic comments https://webpack.js.org/api/module-methods/#magic-comments with `webpackMode: "lazy"`
author: alexander-akait

body:
Hey @alexander-akait !

Unfortunately, `dependOn` is not an option as it is not that dynamic and needs adjustments in the `webpack.config` itself. I gave `webpackMode: "lazy"` a try, but unfortunately it does not work as expected: https://stackblitz.com/edit/github-uymwfx-mvoaar?file=packages/package-b/src/myFunc.js

```
import { myFunc } from '../../package-b/index.js'
```

This is not correct. The file is imported through the dependency name:

```
import { myFunc } from "package-b"
```
author: matzeeable

body:
> you should not rely on a chunk creation mechanism

Why you need it? Webpack has a good optimization logic, so moduke can be merged/concated/remove/etc and chunk will be created depend on it

You can set the same values https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/config/defaults.js#L1129 and have the required chunk, but I **strongly** don't recommend it

body: How many chunks you have? Do you extract runtime? If you have dymanic chunks their names are changed so contenthash is different. Maybe you can provide small example?
author: alexander-akait

body:
> How many chunks you have? Do you extract runtime? If you have dymanic chunks their names are changed so contenthash is different. Maybe you can provide small example?

Thanks for your reply. I found this issue on `monaco-editor` package. It's a third part package, I never change its codes. And I don't know if it used the dymanic chunks within. 
Do you mean the `moduleIds` differences are caused by dymanic chunks? Sure, it could have some additional dymanic chunks in our new codes. And it would cause the `moduleIds` difference indeed.
But I think it should not become a factor to change a file's contenthash which is never been changed, right?
So my question is "Should the contenthash be influenced by moduleIds?" That's the thing I feel weird. 
From my perspective, the calculating `contenthash` process should be earlier than calculating `moduleIds` to ensure the content hash is only influenced by content, not others.

author: alexander-akait

body:
> > I saved my project in two version while their monaco-editor versions are identity.
> 
> what does it means? The important thing is when
> 
> ```js
> import("monaco-editor") /* add a non-initial chunk */
> ```
> 
> happens with same configuration it should have same content hash (if editor was not changed)

Yes, indeed, but it will be changed in the following scenario:
Monday, we released our project named `version1`, the release contained a `monaco.1.js`
Tuesday, some guys add new `dynamic`  modules to the project and released the `version2`. Will it still be calculated to `monaco.1.js`? I think it should be. But the fact is as @alexander-akait  said, it probably was `monaco.2.js` since the `module number changed`.
What is its effect of it? It will let our user's browser cache doesn't work. They need to download `monaco.2.js` when we publish our `version2`. The worse thing is it won't just influence one module. I am not sure how many packages will be influenced.
Any time we add new `dynamic` modules it probably happens. That's why I want to report it. It's not friendly and it's weird. Because I never change the `monaco` version. But its content hash probably is changed.
author: adamma1024

I just used `new MonacoWebpackPlugin({ globalAPI: true })`.
I am not sure that I can provide a reproducible test repo. As we all know, the dependency's module id won't be changed at any time. And I have not found a way to repro it.
But I can promise that I will provide it to you as long as I create a reproducible repo.
author: adamma1024

body:
> if you have something like 1st webpack config with one `moduleIds` ( result in 1st content hash ) and change webpack config to another `moduleIds` => this will change content hash since module ids alaso a part of content..

The webpack's config was never changed. But the module id in Monaco has been changed. Just like:
```
//monaco.js
import A from 'A'
```
After compiled:
```
import A from [1] // 1 is module id
```
If we import a new dynamic module it probably be that:
```
import A from [2] // Since the 1 is taken by the dynamic module.
```
So the content hash will be changed when it happens.

body:
@alexander-akait managed to get a minimal reproduction here: https://github.com/ryanwilsonperkin/webpack-16160
This is just using the image minimizing plugin as well as the filesystem cache. Full reproduction steps in the readme of that repo, but I can now reliably reproduce this by rebuilding after making any change to the SVG asset that's being bundled. I suspect that the loader is using contenthash in one place and filename hash in another
author: ryanwilsonperkin

body: Further discovery, by dropping a couple of console logs in the `AssetModulesPlugin.js` I was able to find that the `data` object is always `undefined` for this asset, it's just that when it's on the second build (after a modification) then the `module.buildInfo.fullContentHash` is also undefined, which causes it to fall through to trying to access the `data.get` method
author: ryanwilsonperkin

body: Interestingly, if I install the `imagemin-pngquant` library and add "png" to the list of files that I want to pass through the minifier, it _does not_ cause errors across re-builds, so this does seem to be specific to SVG files.

body:
In the case of `asset/inline` the bundler would take the asset and inline it into the source as a data URI. If we were using `new URL("./image.svg", import.meta.url)` then i don't think that would work correctly because it would be trying to treat that inlined URI as a relative url with path name. Similar issue for `asset/source`

> Anyway I marked it as a bug, because we should fix it

Thanks! I'm also looking into it, although I'm new to the webpack source code

author: alexander-akait

body:
After digging quite a lot more, I found that, in `lib/sharing/ConsumeSharedRuntimeModule.js`, replacing:

```
for (const chunk of this.chunk.getAllInitialChunks()) {
```

With:

```
for (const chunk of this.chunk.getAllReferencedChunks()) {
```

Fixed the issue in the workers (both the test scenario and real world scenario). I'm sure this might not be the correct approach, but maybe those get functions are not properly picking up the required information on a worker context (where most likely everything should be an "initial chunk"), most likely due to the unique way worker entrypoints are generated.
author: edusperoni

body:
Ok, I think I found the final issue, but i'm not sure if this is something that we should handle in WMF or the "rest" of webpack.

1. Worker is created in lib/dependencies/WorkerPlugin.js as an `AsyncDependenciesBlock`  https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/9fcaa243573005d6fdece9a3f8d89a0e8b399613/lib/dependencies/WorkerPlugin.js#L290
2. lib/buildChunkGraph.js will then call `addAsyncEntrypoint` and `connectBlockAndChunkGroup` https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/9fcaa243573005d6fdece9a3f8d89a0e8b399613/lib/buildChunkGraph.js#L412 https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/9fcaa243573005d6fdece9a3f8d89a0e8b399613/lib/buildChunkGraph.js#L443
3. this entrypoint is created with `initial: false` https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/9fcaa243573005d6fdece9a3f8d89a0e8b399613/lib/Compilation.js#L3626
4. the worker is not considered an initial entrypoint, so no WMF sharing information is added to its runtime.

So the solution might be: add another flag to the `AsyncDependenciesBlock` that either makes it a initial entrypoint (affects webpack as a whole), or adds another flag to the `Entrypoint` that WMF can query when gathering what should/shouldn't be included in the Runtime.

Edit: setting it as initial has some side effects (like duplicated code), but it seems to be an optimization option. I'll wait for people smarter than me to weigh in on this haha
author: edusperoni

body:
What about setting runtime: false on module federation plugin?

If you have runtimeChunk: single - it can cause some issues - I've seen similar problems with webpack ESM output when there's multiple entry points and a single runtime chunk
author: ScriptedAlchemy

body:
@ScriptedAlchemy setting runtime false makes no difference in the output. The worker will always have it's own runtime regardless of setting, but WMF will not add the needed information in it due to the things in my previous comment.

This probably happens because the WMF plugins check only for the initial bundles to add that information in, which a worker isn't, but should be considered one anyway.

body:
I have successfully used MF in SharedWorker without relying on the fix PR https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/18859.

https://github.com/unadlib/webpack-mf-workers

### How to fix

1. Use SharedWorker instead of Worker in host application
2. Dynamically import bootstrap file in worker thread
3. Use promise based dynamic remotes `host/webpack.host-config.js`
4. Use multiple entry points in webpack config `module/webpack.module-config.js`

body:
Hm, you can do it using:
```
externals: {
    "http://somehwere.com/module.js": "import http://somehwere.com/module.js"
},
```

By default webpack hoists them, i.e. `module` https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/config/defaults.js#L222.

The `externalType` type is applied only when `externals` is not empty (bu default it is empty) and `externalType` is the `module` by default for ESM, so you need to specify which external should be loaded using `import(...)`, but yes, logic is strange, I think we should improve it and keep `import(...)` if developer doesn't specify another value

body:
@eps1lon That is intresting, because there is no bug here. Set `type: "javascript/esm"` and you will see it.

Why? Webpack tries by default parse source code using `javascript/auto` (i.e. firstly `javascript/dynamic` then  `javascript/esm` if an exception was thrown). The `strictExports` option works only for  `javascript/esm`. But when a file is empty, webpack successfully parses a file as `javascript/dynamic`, because it was parsed as not `javascript/esm` (i.e ECMA modules) you didn't get errors.

There are two solutions:
1. Parse files as `javascript/esm` firstly (it will be breaking change, we are considering it for webpack v6)
2. Set `javascript/esm` for loaders (reccomended) https://webpack.js.org/configuration/module/#ruletype
author: alexander-akait

author: alexander-akait

body:
I don't get what Webpacks's notion of "type" has anything to do with it other than explaining the implementation. This is just an implementation detail for me. 

I understand you don't want to change the behvavior for the default but if I set `strictExports`, I want `strictExports`. It doesn't matter to me wether Webpack considers empty files a different type. JavaScript does not and that's what `strictExports` should be for. Otherwise we created another migration step that will just cause frustration in the ecosystem: CommonJS sloppyness, Webpack `strictExports` and JS modules. Webpack is injecting itself into the ecosystem when all there should be are CommonJS sloppyness for historic reasons and JS modules.
author: eps1lon

body:
You can do it, just set `type: "javascript/esm"` and webpack will do it as you expected. We can't change logic for `type: "javascript/dynamic"`, we don't know original type of JS/TS when we parse them, so when you have empty file we don't know is it ESM or is it CommonJS, you can `require` empty file, even more you can add export in `setTimeout` and another dirty/hacky ways, so we can't report about these errors. There is only one solution - parse files as ESM first, but it will be breaking change for webpack v5 and sorry we can't change it right now.

---

I am not saying that you are wrong, I just want to point out the technical difficulty of solving this right now without breaking change and I recommend a solution that will help solve this.
author: alexander-akait

body:
Thinking some more on this: most of the false positives here would be resolved if there was a better way to disambiguate context modules and treat their pipe-separated components differently.

Which to me raises the question: why are context modules building all this information into the module path, when factually it can't even be well-represented as part of an URI path to begin with? Regexes don't exactly lend themselves well to being represented as a path segment without judicious URI-encoding being applied. And it's worse than average case here, considering these regexes are actually _meant_ to partially match file paths - meaning characters like slashes are pretty much guaranteed to be present.

Why then not go for the obvious:
Don't use the path to represent that information. Use the _query_ or possibly even the fragment instead.
E.g. in case of the above case where things error:
`./images/* ?sync|/[\\/](?:brands%7Cregion-flags)[\\/]/i` or
`./images/* #sync|/[\\/](?:brands%7Cregion-flags)[\\/]/i`

This would have the added benefit of also the source map navigation in browser developer tools not mistaking the regex parts as part of a path anymore.

Alternatively; I wonder if just URI-encoding slashes would work.
I'm going to try that...
author: rjgotten

body:
That totally worked.
My generated source map now contains what I would expect, with - of course - the regex-specific path separator characters all escaped:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/442808/191027592-bfdb647f-a176-474c-8221-9b3e35d73053.png)

I mean, it's _ugly_ ... but I expect browser developer tools will at least show the URI-decoded result so it should be good.

Here's [a gist of the thing](
https://gist.github.com/rjgotten/6b74fe24800a014c91ff87491ca83ba8) implemented as a plugin which hot-patches the `_prettyRegExp` function to do all the necessary escaping.

body:
also, there is another issue (I updated the minimal repo to address it)

if file1.ts requires 'sub/file2' whitch in its turn requires './file3' (i.e ROOT/sub/file3.ts)
and we added /file3/ into externals[] list.

the scenario would be as following:
file1.js includes the compiled content of file2.js as it, whitch is expected
but file2.js content is `reuire('./file3')` which is excluded from the bundling
here is the problem, the final result of file1.js is `require('./file3') instead of `require('./sub/file3') which is wrong

and the context for this externals is something looks like:

```
{
  context: 'C:\\Users\\w.i\\Desktop\\tmp\\node\\issue-webpack-externals\\src\\sub',
  request: './file3',
  dependencyType: 'esm',
  contextInfo: {
    issuer: 'C:\\Users\\w.i\\Desktop\\tmp\\node\\issue-webpack-externals\\src\\sub\\file2.ts',
    issuerLayer: null,
    compiler: undefined
  },
  getResolve: [Function: getResolve]
}
```
here is file2 is the issuer, but actually as it merged into file1, the real issuer should be file1

@alexander-akait

This is interesting!

If I understand correctly,

- you have one npm module and you want to add this module to each entry. Then what's the reson for using SplitChunks? 
- Or, may be you want to just add lodash to each entries and other modules (in future) to be part of Vendor?

if the use case is the 2nd one then why not adding `test: /[\\/]node_modules[\\/]!(lodash)[\\/]/`
author: pixforger

body:
Hey @rahulkumar26, in reality both entrypoints depend on many more npm modules than just `lodash`. I used only `lodash` in the example to keep things simple.

I want to have a separate vendors chunk for the `app` entry to take advantage of long-term caching. But the way Webpack loads additional chunks seems to be incompatible with service worker, which is why the service worker must be a single JS file.
author: srmagura

body: You can't do it, because it is a limitation, you can move service worker bundling to own compilation (i.e. multi compiler mode with `target: 'webworker'`), otherwise webpack just don't know how to load chunk

If you want you can change `chunks: "all"` to `chunks: "initial"`

author: srmagura

body:
For anyone who may be reading this — the layers thing _seemed_ to be working but actually broke parts of our app. Specifically, we were seeing React context values not being passed down correctly to code in dynamically-loaded chunks. My best guess is that the React JavaScript got included in multiple chunks.

We've switched to just using a separate Webpack config for the service worker, since this seems less error prone and easier to understand.
author: srmagura

body: @srmagura Can you provide example of the problem?
author: alexander-akait
srmagura

body: @srmagura Sound like you have a module which used react-dom in the service worker and in the app, is it right?
author: alexander-akait

body: No, react-dom is never imported by the service worker. The app is split into multiple chunks, and there are multiple chunks that import react-dom.
author: srmagura

body: @srmagura Is it possible that another module which used in service worker uses `react-dom`? Can you define https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/config/defaults.js#L1196 and set `layer` for this too? Also set `default: false` and `defaultVendors: false`, so we disable the default split chunks in favor our logic with layer app (if you need vendor for service worker or dynamic chunks you can set another layer, i.e. service worker)
author: alexander-akait
srmagura

body: Oh, and I'm positive that the service worker does not depend on react-dom whatsoever. It imports a few `workbox` packages and that is it.
author: srmagura

body:
@srmagura Another idea - you have a component/module which import `react-dom` with another version, if you provide reproducible test repo I can investigate it and say that is wrong here (you can send me it on email, if you want I can sign CLA/NDA)

Also can you modify:
```
vendor: {
  layer: layer => !layer || layer === 'app',
  test: /[\\/]node_modules[\\/]/,
  name: 'vendor',
  chunks: 'initial',
},
```

to

```
vendor: {
  layer: layer => !layer || layer === 'app',
  test: /[\\/]node_modules[\\/]/,
  name: 'vendor',
  // or `async`
  chunks: 'all',
  reuseExistingChunk: true,
  priority: -10
},
```
author: alexander-akait

body:
I changed the code to:

```js
vendor: {
  layer: layer => !layer || layer === 'app',
  test: /[\\/]node_modules[\\/]/,
  name: 'vendor',

  chunks: 'all',
  reuseExistingChunk: true,
  priority: -10,
},
```

and now react-dom is only included in the big vendor chunk 🙂.

Then I tried changing `chunks` from `all` back to `initial`. Now `react-dom` is included in two chunks, which will cause issues at runtime.

We are going to continue compiling the service worker via separate webpack config since this seems less likely to cause issues. That said, I think I could create a minimal repro of this if that would be helpful.

(Note to self: `6acaf7a75`)
author: srmagura

body: @srmagura Yeah, will be great to see the minimum reproducible example, so I will answer why it happens

body: Not related to webpack, you should build `@webpack-typescript-references/env` firstly, if you want to use it late, webpack is not `orchestrator` and don't know which dependecies should be build fisrtly
author: alexander-akait

body: I understand that webpack is not an orchestrator, but one of the main uses of Typescript References (and the `--build` flag) is to be able to build dependent projects implicitly, without needing to manually build all dependent projects in the reference tree. I think it would be very intuitive and useful if webpack incorporated support for that aspect of Typescript for it to claim full Typescript support. In essence, it would not be webpack doing the orchestration, it would just be webpack respecting typescripts abilities to do the orchestration for it.
author: aboe026

body: I don't see how we can implement this, we don't know your graph and don't know what depends on what
author: alexander-akait

body: Typescript abstracts it, you don't need to know the graph, all webpack would need to do is run a `tsc --build` before ingesting the `webpack.config.ts`, the `tsc --build` command would automatically build the graph of the workspace so all/any dependent projects referenced in the `webpack.config.ts` would be properly built.
author: aboe026

body: Sorry, not it doesn't work as you write, you can have big monorepo and don't use all libraries inside your application, also we don't know which package should be build first, you can have very complex graph, if we will run `tsc --build` before you will get the HUGE perfomance degradation, you can look at `bazel` if you want to desribe how to build your deps, even more you can use `babel` or `swc` to run new code on new version, and we can't know it, and there are a lot of other limitations
author: alexander-akait

body:
`you can have big monorepo and don't use all libraries inside your application`
Typescript is actually very smart about what it builds and only includes projects in the direct dependency graph.

`also we don't know which package should be build first`
Webpack does not need to know anything about the order of typescript reference dependencies, its all abstracted by Typescript.

`you will get the HUGE perfomance degradation`
Typescript actually runs a difference check and only builds projects that have either not been built or have changes since their last build. So the performance impact should not be that large, especially after the first build.

In any case, it could be an optional opt-in flag so it won't impact any users unless the explicitly desire the functionality. Unless there is already some existing "pre-compile" flag that allows users to specify a command to run before the `webpack.config.ts` is ingested that I'm not aware of, I think it would be a very valuable functionality to add.
author: aboe026

body:
> Typescript is actually very smart about what it builds and only includes projects in the direct dependency graph.

But we don't have ability to undestand it, just running `tsc --build` will have big problem with perfomance

> Webpack does not need to know anything about the order of typescript reference dependencies, its all abstracted by Typescript.

And which library should be build firstly? I don't know

> Typescript actually runs a difference check and only builds projects that have either not been built or have changes since their last build. So the performance impact should not be that large, especially after the first build.

No all projects uses incremental build, some projects add more flags to `tsc --build`, some projects uses babel/swc as I written above, some projects have more complex building and not only run `ts`.

> In any case, it could be an optional opt-in flag so it won't impact any users unless the explicitly desire the functionality. Unless there is already some existing "pre-compile" flag that allows users to specify a command to run before the webpack.config.ts is ingested that I'm not aware of, I think it would be a very valuable functionality to add.

You can just put own command before run `webpack`, that is why you have `scripts` in `package.json`

No one bundler or other tools do it, it is out of scope webpack
author: alexander-akait

body:
`You can just put own command before run webpack, that is why you have scripts in package.json`
As you can see in the reproduction repository I linked in the initial comment that is exactly what I am doing to work around the issue. It would just be nice if the webpack integration with typescript did that for users natively.

`No one bundler or other tools do it, it is out of scope webpack`
I disagree, as webpack is well known for the variety of plugins that are written for it. I was hoping to be fix this issue with a plugin initially (such as the [webpack-shell-plugin-next](https://www.npmjs.com/package/webpack-shell-plugin-next) to do the `tsc --build` for me), but the problem is I need the compilation (`tsc --build`) to happen before the `webpack.config.ts` is even read. Is there any mechanism to do this? I feel like it would be extremely useful for users, and not just restricted to my own use case. Something like a `--pre-config` flag to run any arbitrary command before the config file is read in?
I'm not sure how that would ever have a negative impact on a user since it is opt-in, so any "slowdown" would be self-inflicted by the user themselves, and it would be up for them to decide if the ability to do something is worth the tradeoff in slowness (in my case, I would prefer to actually be able to do something rather than nothing, even if it comes with the slight performance hit that needs to come anyways, regardless of where the `tsc --build` command happens).
author: aboe026

You should manually invalidate cache in this case, webpack can't do it for you, you can use https://webpack.js.org/configuration/cache/#cacheversion and store version of dependeices (hash of all urls or something else), if you have ability to use filesystem (for example remote disk), you can put paths here https://webpack.js.org/configuration/cache/#cachebuilddependencies

body:
Thanks for the answer!

Can you please elaborate a bit about `cache.buildDependencies`? This is a list of file or something else? Can I add `package-lock.json` to it in some way?
author: maksbotan

body: This is a list of files, webpack stores snapshots of them (i.e. timestamp and hash of content, also if it is js, webpack parses them and include all `require`/`import` in deps, too under the hood), so any changes in these files invalidate your build, by default we store this here - https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/config/defaults.js#L364 and webpack-cli by default add `webpakc.config.js` to this list too
author: alexander-akait

body:
Well, this is very confusing behavior. I've set up my CI to store webpack cache between builds. Do I understand correctly that each time I update some of my dependency's version, I have to manually clean that cache?

Would it make sense instead to add `package-lock.json` to `cache.buildDependencies`? Or at least mention this in the docs? I can make PR for that.
author: maksbotan

body:
> Do I understand correctly that each time I update some of my dependency's version, I have to manually clean that cache?

Webpack by default get your `package.json` and store infromation, but if you want to change default `buildDependencies`, you need to include `package.json`, so developer can create any own cache strategy. You can run webpack in watch mode with cache and change `package.json` (and install new deps or update and you will see - webpack compile the new version).

All webpack cache based on snapshot logic - https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/config/defaults.js#L382 and you can see that we consider your version and updates (cache based on this logic too)
author: alexander-akait

body:
Sorry, I do not follow.

When I set, according to webpack documentation (https://webpack.js.org/configuration/cache/#cachebuilddependencies), `cache.buildDependencies: [__filename]` I __disable__ default behavior of depending on `package.json`?

Or if I don't, why it doesn't work?
author: maksbotan

body: webpack-cli by default add `webpack.config.js` to `buildDependencies`, shortly - `buildDependencies` is list of files on which build depends, so if you have custom webpack runner you should put there all files, `package.json` will be considered in any way because `snapshot` logic depends on it
author: alexander-akait

body:
I have a hypothesis about why I get this behavior. I have a dependency on git _branch_, not a specific commit hash. So anytime I push to that branch and update the dependency, I get changes only in `package-json.lock`, __not__ in `package.json` itself.

May it be the case?
author: maksbotan

body: Yes, so you need to use `version` to detect it, anyway if you have changes in `package-json.lock`, you can put them in `buildDependencies` too

body:
Usually, it can set [remotes.shareScope](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/schemas/plugins/container/ModuleFederationPlugin.json#L314) to specify the shareScope that initializes the shared in `host` ,and set [shareScope](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/schemas/plugins/container/ModuleFederationPlugin.json#L504) in  `producer`. 

But in this case ,it load remote module dynamic . And the `loadRemoteModule` will automatic init remote shared module by setting shareScope as `'default'` ,which will cause `mfe2` can't use `mfe3`'s shared module 。

You can load remote module and then set specify shareScope to init shared module manually. More detail [view this](https://github.com/2heal1/Module-Federation-Example/commit/69bf8c58c77789a63b3178933c2142df5d12b041) .

And I found, `ContainerPlugin` has shareScope option , but `ModuleFederationPlugin` not set shareScope to `ContainerPlugin`. So I send PR #16407 .

Summarize, I solve this problem by using #16407 combined with setting [specify shareScope](https://github.com/2heal1/Module-Federation-Example/commit/69bf8c58c77789a63b3178933c2142df5d12b041) .

hope this can be helpful

body:
All remotes need to be initialized upfront. Otherwise you run the risk of partially patching share scope into another runtime. We can't reinitialize a remote, so at point or initialization, that's the only keys the remote has, if you add more remotes after the fact - they can assign shares to the scope, but runtimes already initialized cannot access these new shares. 

There are some ways around this - however it might lead to unexpected behavior which is why we seal the scope at point of initialization per runtime. 

You could use an es proxy 

Remote.init(new Proxy(webpackSharescope)) and add a handler to it. Then the object never "changes" but when querying scope you can get the latest memory references with updated keys.

Relevant points in the [asset documentation](https://webpack.js.org/guides/asset-modules/):

> Asset Modules is a type of module that allows one to use asset files (fonts, icons, etc) without configuring additional loaders.

> Prior to webpack 5 it was common to use:
> ...
> [file-loader](https://v4.webpack.js.org/loaders/file-loader/) to emit a file into the output directory

> asset/resource emits a separate file and exports the URL. Previously achievable by using file-loader.

Also, the following doesn't work, having tried it as well:

> To exclude assets that came from new URL calls from the asset loaders add dependency: { not: ['url'] } to the loader configuration.

@alexander-akait , thank you for your help on this PR.  I've looked around the web, and was able to determine what @ScriptedAlchemy referred to.  There's an example of using delegated modules in the module federation examples:
https://github.com/module-federation/module-federation-examples/tree/master/delegate-modules

By adopting this approach (I actually combined a variation of the code in [promised based dynamic remotes](https://webpack.js.org/concepts/module-federation/#promise-based-dynamic-remotes)) with the code in the above sample, I was able to achieve my desired functionality while also making the build output a lot cleaner.

As a result of this, I don't think my PR is needed, and it can be closed.

Thanks for you reply.@alexander-akait . I test async import before. It seems it could not be shaken #16271 #14800.
May I ask why do you think the best place for it is terser repo? Webpack seems already has done some elimination work before let terser handles it. For example , for the original code I posted, all code after "false" is already removed, so chunk "bundle_mobile" not be added to chunk graph in the first place. Isn't get removed on purpose? Does splitchunk happens before terser? Even terser plugin can eliminate it, wouldn't it be too late, cause "Baz" is already added to binding part in the vendors chunk and terser plugin only works on chunk level. Why some code is already removed before terser? Why code after "break" statement cannot be part of it?
author: icy0307

body: Because it is DCE (dead code elimination), it is not a part of webpack, webpack just says `false`/`undefined`/etc or remove usage (+ import/export) and then terser remove everything which was not used, i.e. do DCE

body:
webpack can't undestand what code is in `Promise.resolve($)`, so we can't `awai` it twice, when you have:
```js
const jq = await Promise.resolve($);
jq('.my-element').animate(/* ... */);
```

`$` is promise
and `Promise.resolve` is promise too

So you have Promise inside Promise, that is why I said you need to do it on application side (i.e. resolve) or hack webpack using a custom plugin.

You see `__unused_webpack_module` due above reasons, webpack can't handle such things, `Promise.resolve($)` is just a string, not more

> The docs do not mention limitations or behavior variances, so I'm not sure what you're referring to.

Yes, we don't have all possible limitations in docs (mostly because no one use it in such way and some of them are really very very rare).
author: alexander-akait

body:
I understand what you're saying, but in the case of my example repo, none of the parameters passed to `Promise.resolve` are themselves promises. For example, in the case of `Promise.resolve(React)`, `React` is not a promise, but a `React` object. So none of these things would need to be double-awaited. I'm using `Promise.resolve` as a substitute for more complex code that boils down to the same thing when it comes time to require it in webpack—the `externals` are Promises (not Promises within Promises) that need to be awaited.

> You see __unused_webpack_module due above reasons, webpack can't handle such things, Promise.resolve($) is just a string, not more

`__unused_webpack_module` is _not_  being applied to my external packages, though. It's being applied to dependencies that the webpack tree should know about in more detail than the externals rendered with `Promise.resolve`.

In the case of the example repo, `react-dom` is a _non-externalized_ dependency that itself has a dependency on one of the externalized packages (`'react'`). Technically speaking, `react-dom` is _used_, but is erroneously getting marked as unused. Even without any `externals` configured at all, that package still has `__unused_webpack_module`. So `Promise.resolve` _cannot_ be the thing causing it to be marked as unused.

But that whole discussion seems to be a tangent from the issue itself, which is that _all modules_ marked as unused are doing the _wrong_ thing with `externalsType: promise`. None of them are awaiting the external dependency. It's not a case of my configuration necessitating a double `await`, it's a case of the _first_ `await` not happening at all.
author: stevematney

body:
> I understand what you're saying, but in the case of my example repo, none of the parameters passed to Promise.resolve are themselves promises. For example, in the case of Promise.resolve(React), React is not a promise, but a React object. So none of these things would need to be double-awaited. I'm using Promise.resolve as a substitute for more complex code that boils down to the same thing when it comes time to require it in webpack—the externals are Promises (not Promises within Promises) that need to be awaited.

But you have `externalsType: 'promise',`, so webpack wraps them as promise (look at generated runtime).

> But that whole discussion seems to be a tangent from the issue itself, which is that all modules marked as unused are doing the wrong thing with externalsType: promise. None of them are awaiting the external dependency. It's not a case of my configuration necessitating a double await, it's a case of the first await not happening at all.

You want inject promised external deps in non promised code (and plus added more `Prmose.resolve()`, I'm not sure that I can say that this is generally a good idea, and expect `await`ing them), sorry, it really was not design for such cases. If you really need this, you need to handle it on an application side (using `import(...)` and await required dependecies) or you can change webpack runtime using an own custom plugin.
author: alexander-akait

body:
> You want inject promised external deps in non promised code...

This is what using `externalsType: promise` already does. My original modules may or may not be written with promises, but Webpack handles awaiting the externals by basically rewriting my original modules into async methods.

I noticed that this `async` rewrite _also_ happens with third-party code that is written as non-async ES Modules. This lead me to think that maybe it was the module _type_ that allowed for awaiting promises. It turns out, that is correct. I wrote a local React component as a commonjs module rather than an ES Module, and that code also does not `await` the external dependency.

So, to be clear:
This file works: https://github.com/stevematney/webpack-promise-externals-bug-example/blob/main/src/index.js
This file does not: https://github.com/stevematney/webpack-promise-externals-bug-example/blob/main/src/CommonJsTest.js
author: stevematney

body:
> ...and plus added more `Promise.resolve()`...

I want to be clear: there are no extra Promises involved. We're only talking about awaiting a single Promise. We're _never_ talking about resolving a Promise to another Promise, and expecting webpack to know how to await that secondary Promise. I'm saying that I expect `externalsType: 'promise'` to work as described, and it does not seem to do so with modules that are not esm.

body: What do you try to solve? Why don't use `externals` as a function?
author: alexander-akait

body:
> What do you try to solve? Why don't use externals as a function?

Sorry, we can definitely use externals as a function, but we can't use that function to sometimes return a Promise and sometimes _not_ return a Promise. We're doing a few different things:
1. Loading externals asynchronously via UMD urls
2. Running multiple bundles on a page, but sharing the loaded text of the externals between bundles by processing them on a context object isolated to each bundle rather than on the global scope
3. Ensuring that we only request an external a single time over the network
4. Using webpack's `promise` external type to deliver those externals at runtime, allowing us to run bundles _as the externals are loading_, and for that code to only pause while the external is loaded.

The [isolated-externals-plugin README](https://github.com/WTW-IM/isolated-externals-plugin#usage) explains what we're doing and why more completely.

body: Just an update: we worked around this issue in our plugin by using a special loader which gets applied if any of the parents of an external are non-esm. The loader preloads the externals into a synchronous object before running the original entry code. The downstream dependencies are overwritten to reference this synchronous object _instead of_ the Promised externals.
