body:
> We use webpack in jest test suites - have a lot of small SSR applications for testing specific features, and for every test suite in setup hooks we build this application once.

I think it makes sense to change the approach, such testing does not look good in terms of performance

Why you can bundle everything before tests and cache it? It will be faster also allow to cache it on CI
author: alexander-akait

body:
> Why you can bundle everything before tests and cache it? It will be faster also allow to cache it on CI

There is a custom dev server, useful for SSR apps, because we can reuse existed CLI command for development where server and client bundles will be created, and server started

For example, looks like Next.js use similar approach for e2e tests:
- test suite - https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/test/e2e/streaming-ssr/index.test.ts#L19
- dev utils - https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/canary/test/lib/next-modes/next-dev.ts#L20
(but we not spawn different processes in tests, maybe it is a good idea)

And out tests:
- test suite - https://github.com/Tinkoff/tramvai/blob/main/examples/assets/__integration__/application.test.ts#L12
- dev utils - https://github.com/Tinkoff/tramvai/blob/main/packages/tramvai/test/integration/src/app/startCli.ts#L38
author: SuperOleg39

body:
I see, I think we can implement this using a different name, but it requires to rewrite many things, because our hooks are async for cache for better perf. 

In theory it can be solved by a custom plugin, because `idle` is just a plugin https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/cache/IdleFileCachePlugin.js#L15, so you can disable cache and enable cache using plugins (like here https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/WebpackOptionsApply.js#L623) in your configuration with own idle plugin

I faced recently a similar scenario: the error was caused by a syntax error in safari due to a lookbehind regex (not supported in safari).
The chunk was downloaded successfully but an exception was triggered during script evaluation and reported by webpack runtime as loading error.

I can share a reproducible example if needed and glad to take a look at it and send a PR!
author: aespinolopez

body:
Hi @alexander-akait 
I created a [reproducible repo](https://github.com/aespinolopez/webpack_script_evaluation_error) for my comment above.

Both the parsing error and webpack chunk error are reported to console and the orignal error should always be displayed by the [specificatoin](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/webappapis.html#creating-scripts) if i understand it correctly.
The parsing error will be opaque in case of cross origin scripts and it will log Script Error.

In order to add the original error to ChunkLoadError a global error handler is needed and i don't know if this is a good idea (i'm open to suggestions but i think it is the only possible implementation) so my suggestion would be to change the error message of ChunkLoadError IF my assumptions are correct:

Webpack reports the error on **lib/web/JsonpChunkLoadingRuntimeModule.js:177**. This callback is attached to the script onload/onerror handlers and only runs if **installedChunks** is truthy (only possible truthy value is [res, rej, promise]) meaning the chunk has not load correctly.
In this scenario if the handler for the callback is onload the error type is set to **missing** and i think this is misleading and incorrect because the only possible scenario here is that the script has actually being loaded but an exception was thown during parsing / evaluation otherwise installedChunkData will be 0.

As conclusion i think the log  "missing: {url}" should be change to a value like ChunkError: {url}
author: aespinolopez

body: @aespinolopez sorry for delay, if you are intresting you can send a PR and I can help you
author: alexander-akait

body:
@alexander-akait sure, glad to send a PR, but i don't know which is the preferred solution. 

- 1. Changing the wording of the error message to reflect a runtime error during evaluation / execution
- 2. Looking for the real error message to report it in webpack's runtime
- some alternative solution

Hey folks, I encountered same issue in my app and found something interesting which could explain randomness.

- I have chunk CSS (`my-chunk.css`) which imports fonts from google fonts like this `@import url('https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Inter:wght@100;200;300;400;500;600;700;800;900&display=swap');`
- and if I block `fonts.googleapis.com` domain, I get ChunkLoadError with information that `my-chunk.css` failed to be loaded even I see that it was loaded fine in network

And as far as I know, googleapis is blocked in some countries (e.g. China) or can be blocked by some firewalls or maybe even browser extensions because Google tracks user IP address.

If you want to use the each entry as separated page you need to configure https://webpack.js.org/configuration/optimization/#optimizationruntimechunk.

Set:
```
runtimeChunk: {

and

```
<script src="./dist/runtime.js"></script>
<script src="./dist/vendors.js"></script>
<script src="./dist/common.js"></script>
<script src="./dist/c.js"></script>

To avoid writting it manually I recommend to use `html-webpack-plugin`

body: Hi, I've been working on this PR and have some thoughts regarding asynchronous computations. If we want to support asynchronous computation here, we'd need to declare fn as an async function where it's run in the source code, specifically in `lib/DefinePlugin.js`, use await when calling it, and ensure the upstream functions are also async. However, this would lead to the "infectious" nature of asynchronous functions, where fn being async would force the top-level functions to also be async. I've noticed that the current design seems to assume these operations are synchronous. For example, consider the declaration for `evaluateIdentifier`: > > You can refactor code and make it async > > This shouldn't be possible to implement, because all JavascriptParser Hooks are sync, and can't handle async operations. These suggestions from @sepenlei may be right: Therefore, if we were to support asynchronous operations, the declarations here would likely need to be redesigned. I'm also unsure if such changes would impact other modules. If you believe this modification and line of thought are worthwhile, I'm willing to give it a try. However, before I proceed, I'd greatly appreciate your ideas on these proposed changes and the experimental approach.

body:
I did some more digging here, and don't think the problem is due to the ESM module being cached due to earlier imports. 
I tried removing the other `swr` imports, and the require-call in my imported commonjs module still receives the module and not main export from `swr`. 
The problem seems to be webpack picking the `swr` `module` export, even when `swr` is being loaded via a CommonJS require call..
author: ToshB

body:
I think this problem is caused by webpack preferring 'module' over 'main' (due to my resolve.mainFields). While the exports field is directly connected to how the module is requested, `import` or `require`, which can give a mix of ESM-exports being provided to CommonJS `require`-calls. 
I can override this behavior by changing `resolve.mainFields` or `resolve.conditionNames`, but it's unfortunate that it's changed for my entire build and not just the case where I'm requiring modules from CommonJS code. 

I think I'll put in a vote for the `swr` [issue requesting named export,](https://github.com/vercel/swr/pull/1889) which will fix my issue.
author: ToshB

body: I solved this by adding a module condition to the package.json exports fields in my modules, which causes webpack to pick up the ESM export.

body:
@alexander-akait I think I found that this works so will close this and leave this here for someone else. My "fixes" were:


1. Add bigger stack-size

```
"start": "npx rimraf build/development && node --max-old-space-size=16384 --stack-size=1000 node_modules/webpack/bin/webpack serve --live-reload --open --config ./webpack.config.dev.js",
```

2. Plus i updated the deps to this

```json
   "html-webpack-plugin": "^5.6.3",
    "webpack": "5.98.0",
    "webpack-bundle-analyzer": "^4.10.2",
    "webpack-cli": "^6.0.1",
    "webpack-dev-server": "^5.2.0",
```

3. Also i got rid of `mini-css-extract-plugin` dependency in my web pack config.

Between these three things it seems resolved but i think it was the `--stack-size=1000 ` addition.

body:
Happened to me recently when I patched our application to Angular v20.3.18:
```
./node_modules/papaparse/papaparse.min.js - Error: Module build failed (from ./node_modules/@angular-devkit/build-angular/src/tools/babel/webpack-loader.js):                                                              
RangeError: /home/.../node_modules/papaparse/papaparse.min.js: Maximum call stack size exceeded 
```

Solution was to update the version of **@babel/helper-define-polyfill-provider** to **v0.6.8**+

body: Yeah, it looks like the parsing issue with `papaparse.min.js` has been fixed [on the Babel side](https://github.com/babel/babel-polyfills/issues/254).

author: hamzahamidi

body:
If the 'models.js' file does exist in the 'src' folder and is being correctly output to the 'dist' folder, then the issue may be with how the module is being imported and used in your application.

Here are a few things you could check:

Make sure that you're importing the module using the correct filename, without the file extension (e.g. import models from './dist/models')
Make sure that you're importing the module from the correct path. If the file is in the 'dist' folder, you'll need to import it using a relative path that starts with './' (e.g. import models from './dist/models')
Make sure that you're using the correct capitalization for the module name. JavaScript is case-sensitive, so 'models' and 'Models' are treated as different modules.
Check your application for any typographical errors in the import path.
If you are still facing the issue, you should check your bundle with webpack-bundle-analyzer, it will help you to see if your models.js is correctly included in your bundle or not.

Please let me know if this helps, or if you have any other questions.

body: As I mentioned in the above parcel issue, I think it is weird that `import.meta.resolve` would _influence_ the resolution rather than only resolving to what is already there.`new URL('...', import.meta.url)` creates a dependency, therefore moving the target module or asset to a separate file/bundle, and it would be weird if `import.meta.resolve` did that IMO. That would mean using `import.meta.resolve` would change the file structure of the output vs not using it. Maybe providing a URL reference to the resolved module within whatever bundle it ends up in via other dependencies would be more expected?

body:
> `new URL('...', import.meta.url)` creates a dependency, therefore moving the target module or asset to a separate file/bundle, and it would be weird if `import.meta.resolve` did that IMO.

I don't see why that would be weird or why they should behave differently. In my view, `import.meta.resolve` is a direct replacement for `new URL('...', import.meta.url)`, just with a proper "meta" API instead of pattern-matching a somewhat brittle JS expression that is matched across tooling only by convention.
author: RReverser

body:
> > `new URL('...', import.meta.url)` creates a dependency, therefore moving the target module or asset to a separate file/bundle, and it would be weird if `import.meta.resolve` did that IMO.
> 
> I don't see why that would be weird or why they should behave differently. In my view, `import.meta.resolve` is a direct replacement for `new URL('...', import.meta.url)`, just with a proper "meta" API instead of pattern-matching a somewhat brittle JS expression that is matched across tooling only by convention.

I strongly agree with this. `import.meta.resolve(…)` was designed as a standardized way to do what some bundlers have been doing by detecting `new URL(…, import.meta.url)`. It also comes with clear semantics for bare module names (i.e. resolving a package rather than a relative path):
https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Operators/import.meta/resolve#comparison_with_new_url

Having a standardized way to do this is absolutely crucial for those of us who maintain packages that include web workers or WASM.

Sorry for delay, we don't create blob by default because it will increase your bundle, you need to create Blob on application side and handle it there, I provide solutions multiple times with different examples and how to handle them, look at dicussions, worker-loader is depcreated and should not be used anymore.

For worker public path we have https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/16671 and it will be in the next release.

> Uncaught DOMException: Failed to construct 'Worker': Script at 'http://localhost:4000/test/src_worker_js.js' cannot be accessed from origin 'http://localhost:3000'.

It should be handled and configured on server side, yes, you can setup proxy for such purposes. Sorry but we can't do something here. Blob is good approach only for old browsers as a fallback.

Also you provided https://medium.com/@krishnachirumamilla/content-security-policy-worker-src-cd06ecfa2fe8, and there is solution, if you need such things you can use multi compiler mode and set `target: 'webworker'` and load your worker sciprt as your want - look at assets modules (`raw` loading), so you will get compiled code and pass it to `Blob`. You can write code based on your logic - blob for dev env and old browser and modern worker for new browsers.

feel free to feedback

If you need I can write you configuration to you but then I need an repository with your real problem, no need to provide whole code, anyway I strongly recommend to look at multi compoer mode with `target: 'webworker'` it is very flexibility for any purposes

body:
> Sorry for delay, we don't create blob by default because it will increase your bundle, you need to create Blob on application side and handle it there, I provide solutions multiple times with different examples and how to handle them, look at dicussions, worker-loader is depcreated and should not be used anymore.

Can you please point out the proper one. There are too many different discussions and advices, it would be great to get the right one from you, since you're the expert.

> For worker public path we have #16671 and it will be in the next release.

Will it work same way as `publicPath: auto`, e.g. path/domain agnostic? I don't know which domain & path the build will be deployed at the time of compilation.

> > Uncaught DOMException: Failed to construct 'Worker': Script at 'http://localhost:4000/test/src_worker_js.js' cannot be accessed from origin 'http://localhost:3000'.
> 
> It should be handled and configured on server side, yes, you can setup proxy for such purposes. Sorry but we can't do something here. Blob is good approach only for old browsers as a fallback.
>
> Also you provided https://medium.com/@krishnachirumamilla/content-security-policy-worker-src-cd06ecfa2fe8, and there is solution, if you need such things you can use multi compiler mode and set `target: 'webworker'` and load your worker sciprt as your want - look at assets modules (`raw` loading), so you will get compiled code and pass it to `Blob`. You can write code based on your logic - blob for dev env and old browser and modern worker for new browsers.

Author of the article says: "Browsers don’t allow you to create a worker with a URL pointing to a different domain.". Chrome ignores the `Content-Security-Policy": "worker-src http://localhost:4000` (different domain) and still throws the above-mentioned error. It seems that you can only allow same-origin workers.

I have tried `target: 'webworker'` — no luck, since it's the problem of Chrome, not compilation. Thus, my statement about necessity to use `blob` even in modern browsers.

I have used following technique:

```js
const myWorker = function createWorker(workerUrl) {
    const blob = new Blob([`importScripts('${workerUrl}');`], {'type': 'application/javascript'});
    return new Worker(URL.createObjectURL(blob));
};
```

Here's the repo: https://github.com/kirill-konshin/cross-domain-worker/tree/target-webworker (note the branch `target-webworker`). In order to see the error uncomment `const worker = new Worker("http://localhost:4000/test/main.js")` and comment `const worker = myWorker("http://localhost:4000/test/main.js")` in `packages/host/src/index.js`.

Unfortunately blob worker throws error when adding `import('./image.png')`: `Uncaught (in promise) DOMException: Failed to execute 'importScripts' on 'WorkerGlobalScope': The script at 'blob:http://localhost:3000/src_image_png.js' failed to load.` — note that it uses host URL, not worker URL, and tries to load `image_png.js` despite the `asset/inline` setting for PNG. It works with regular `require('./image.png')`. I should probably file another bug for this...

> If you need I can write you configuration to you but then I need an repository with your real problem, no need to provide whole code, anyway I strongly recommend to look at multi compoer mode with `target: 'webworker'` it is very flexibility for any purposes

The whole code has been provided: https://github.com/kirill-konshin/cross-domain-worker/tree/original-issue, along with comments in the code and here.
author: kirill-konshin

body:
Looks like I've solved most of the issues, the trick was to explicitly supply a true worker path as `__webpack_public_path__` and `__webpack_require__.p` inside the worker. Still needs a small blob helper to overcome the same-origin policy.

What's supported: 

- cross-domain worker
- no worker loader
- sourcemaps
- dynamic `import()` of images and anything else

https://github.com/kirill-konshin/cross-domain-worker/blob/main/packages/cross-domain-worker/src/index.js I have published the NPM package `cross-domain-worker` since the solution is quite reusable.

https://github.com/kirill-konshin/cross-domain-worker/blob/main/packages/host/src/index.js usage in main thread.

https://github.com/kirill-konshin/cross-domain-worker/blob/main/packages/worker/src/worker.js usage in worker.

body:
I spent quite a while digging before finding this issue. For other people searching, I'll include the error produced by puppeteer, to hopefully lead them straight to an explanation:

```
TypeError: Readable is not a constructor
```

As far as I can tell, this stems from legacy nodejs behavior regarding the `stream`-module. When using `require`, the returned module is a function (this is not the case with `import('stream')`, which is also confusing in and of itself). Since functions are objects too, properties like `Readable` can still be accessed without issue. Without this legacy behavior, modules such as [send](https://www.npmjs.com/package/send), which is used widely in server-libraries, breaks completely, so hacks with externals, alias etc. to only expose the non-legacy exports cannot be used.

The problem arises when webpack tries to create a fake namespace object for the module. Since the module is not an object, but a function, it is assumed that it should instead be treated as the default-export. It's hard to see a simple fix that will allow webpack to create fake namespace objects for legacy modules and not break on this, except if all native modules could be excepted from the fake namespace behavior. Maybe it could look for custom properties on function-exports as well, but there might be tons of ways this could break for other modules.

The solution I ended up with is a god-awful hack, but it works: a babel plugin to inject `/* webpackIgnore: true */`-comments into dynamic imports where the source matches a predefined list. If there's interest, I may publish it as a module, or someone else with experience creating and maintaining babel plugins may do so freely. It's unlikely to have real applications outside of this single issue, but you never know.

```js
/**
 * Add webpackIgnore-comments to dynamic imports matching a specific list.
 * Primarily useful for avoiding problematic handling of the `stream` module by
 * webpack.
 *
 * Provide a list of targeted modules as the `ignore`-option.
 * 
 * Related webpack-issue: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/16757
 */
module.exports = ({ template, types }) => ({
	visitor: {
		Import(path, { opts: { ignore = [] } = {} }) {
			// Check if import statement seems to be completely straightforward, single string argument, no leading comments
			if (path.parent.arguments.length !== 1) return
			const source = path.parent.arguments[0]
			if (!types.isStringLiteral(source) || source.leadingComments) return
			// Check if module should be ignored
			if (!ignore.includes(source.value)) return
			types.addComment(source, 'leading', ' webpackIgnore: true ')
		}
	}
})
```

Usage in babel config (I would recommend a JS config, so `require.resolve` can be used for an absolute path to the plugin-file):

```json
["./path-to-ignore-plugin", { "ignore": ["stream"] }]
```

Make sure that your babel-loader is run on puppeteer-files (and any other modules with the same issue).

    at async PipelineStepSets.perform (/path-to-project/dist/npm/290.js:3459:16)
```

If I add the following configuration in `webpack.config.js`, then there are no issues at all. After observing the compiled output files, it is evident that after adding the configuration, `Puppeteer` and its dependencies are not included in the compilation process, and no compiled files are generated. Instead, the packages are still referenced from the `node_modules` directory. I suspect this is why `nest start` can run without any problems.

```javascript
externals: {
  puppeteer: "require('puppeteer')"
},
```

body:
It looks like this might have something to do with webpack incorrectly compiling `.cjs` files in packages that are `"type": "module"`

In @laustdeleuran's [repo](https://github.com/laustdeleuran/marked-2265-module-import-export-issue), If I remove `"type": "module"` in marked and rename `marked.cjs` to `marked.js` everything works fine.

Detailed Steps to reproduce:

1. Clone repo https://github.com/laustdeleuran/marked-2265-module-import-export-issue
2. Run `pnpm i`
3. Open File `/node_modules/.pnpm/marked@4.2.12/node_modules/marked/package.json`
4. Edit line 24
   ```diff
   - "default": "./lib/marked.cjs"
   + "default": "./lib/marked.js"
   ```
5. Remove line 6
   ```diff
   - "type": "module",
   ```
6. Rename File
   ```diff
   - /node_modules/.pnpm/marked@4.2.12/node_modules/marked/lib/marked.cjs
   + /node_modules/.pnpm/marked@4.2.12/node_modules/marked/lib/marked.js
   ```
7. Run `cd some-cra`
8. Run `pnpm start`

body: When using `RealContentHashPlugin`, there's also a performance opportunity to not have the original contenthash values be hashes at all, but instead be predictably-shaped unique tokens that can be matched as a single pattern (e.g. `__WEBPACK_CONTENT_HASH__${id}`). This would allow matching to be performed by a much simpler RegExp and to skip original generation of `contentHash` (since it will get thrown out anyway).
author: dmichon-msft

body: @dmichon-msft Sounds good idea, but I am afraid it will require to rewrite some plugin outside of webpack ecosystem
author: alexander-akait

body: @dmichon-msft thats not possible.. the hole idea is that you can plug-in/plug-out plugins..
author: vankop

body:
> @dmichon-msft thats not possible.. the hole idea is that you can plug-in/plug-out plugins..

With an appropriate hook in content hash generation, RealContentHashPlugin could override the original content hash generation to account for that it is going to get completely replaced. Alternatively, it could become a standard step in the webpack process that content hashes start as a marker and get replaced during a later hook (that other plugins can tap into). I've used the token-then-replace model in a few plugins to-date because it stabilizes output and makes, e.g. minification more cacheable.

Equally relevant to optimizing this scenario is that the full list of referenced hashes in a given asset comes from the code generation process; we ought to be able to annotate the asset info with which hashes were injected into the asset at the moment they are being injected, without having to do a RegExp search through the final code.

body:
been looking at this since enhanced-resolve#473 - want to try an actual fix instead of just documenting the escape

idea is to update parseResource so if a path is absolute and # is followed by a path separator it treats it as part of the path not a fragment - so ./file.js#fragment and relative context paths still work fine, only absolute paths are affected

this should avoid the regression that caused enhanced-resolve#244 to get reverted since that fix was too broad and broke relative context paths - this one doesn't touch those at all

lmk if this approach makes sense or if there's a reason it won't work, happy to open a pr

Hm intresting, `import.meta.url` in such contest is a part of assets modules, so if you disable evalation of `import.meta.url` here you will disable assets modules at all (except `import "data:..."`), I am afraid some developer can want to have assets modules and disable evalation of `import.meta`, so I think it is not a bug and it is a feature require. But yes, it sounds misleading because you are waiting `import.meta` will be kept as is everywhere.

I think we can't change behaviour of this without breaking chage, so I'd prefer to add a new option - allow to disable assets modules at all, anyway you can manually change your `defaultRules` and don't add this https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/config/defaults.js#L657

body:
I am still experimenting, but one thing that has helped a little is:

- Following/using https://pyperf.readthedocs.io/en/latest/system.html, e.g. setting up a machine where a core is isolated. But if you want to use more than one core, things get really weird based on scheduling, and I'm working on figuring that one out.
- In general, hide benchmark results that are not "significant"; I added to our perf infrastructure something which performs a U-test like https://pkg.go.dev/golang.org/x/perf/benchstat and hides the results which are unlikely to be "real" in that they are indistiguishable from random chance to p=0.05.

There are some other flags I'm testing, specifically `--predictable`, which make benchmarks _super_ comparable, but I'm still working to udnerstand the implications of this flag and if it's not a good idea to use.

Also: https://github.com/faster-cpython/ideas/issues/480

body:
There is a `edit` button on your issue, avoid extra pinning and multipl posting, thank you.

Regarding your issue, you can't split `chunks: "all" when you use module federation right now, even more I strongly recommend don't use `chunks: "all" (and when you don't use module federation),` at all, anyway we can fix it.
 
And the main question, why you want to split remote (especial initial), what is the point? What you expected - many requests for initial chunks, it is not a good idea?
author: alexander-akait

body:
> chunks: "all" when you use module federation right now

which is what i'd like to fix. It's not just chunks: all, but in multiple projects, we use the SplitChunksPlugin to create "vendor" and "common" cache groups for better caching on the browser. Even those don't work when module federation plugin is added

> And the main question, why you want to split remote (especial initial), what is the point?

I am not trying to split remote, just pointing out the difference in the remote entry file that gets generated with and without SplitChunksPlugin, which i believe is the cause of the app crashing in the browser

> What you expected - many requests for initial chunks, it is not a good idea?

For large projects, yes, i do believe this is an issue even with HTTP2 because there is overhead and in HTTP2, the number of parallel requests are limited to 25 (i am not very sure of this number, different sources say different numbers)

In any case, either this is a bug which should be fixed OR a wont-fix that should atleast be documented on the module federation plugin docs

on a side note, can you point out why `chunks: "all"` is bad? because it is explicitly recommended in the code splitting guide in the docs - https://webpack.js.org/guides/code-splitting/#splitchunksplugin
author: burhanuday

body:
> which is what i'd like to fix. It's not just chunks: all, but in multiple projects, we use the SplitChunksPlugin to create "vendor" and "common" cache groups for better caching on the browser. Even those don't work when module federation plugin is added

I can provide a lot of cases, can you provide your case (with minumim code and etc, just for help)

I agree that we have, let's say, a problem and nowhere in the documentation is it said about it.

Shorty - splitting any initial chunks can break your module federation, that is why we have https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/config/defaults.js#L1186 by default.

We can fix by filtering it, like in provided worarkund by you (there is some things were missed and that is why you found that it dones't work for you). 

> on a side note, can you point out why chunks: "all" is bad? because it is explicitly recommended in the code splitting guide in the docs - https://webpack.js.org/guides/code-splitting/#splitchunksplugin

Good catch, we should fix our docs, I will open an issue after we finished this issue, we should recommend to use `default` setting, I think this example is just for example, and it was not particularly apparently assumed that someone was using it, but apparently we were mistaken

author: alexander-akait

body:
> 
> ```
> import(/* webpackPreload: true */ 'ChartingLibrary');
> ```
> When a page which uses the ChartComponent is requested, the charting-library-chunk is also requested via `<link rel="preload">` 

Will the description of Preload in the document cause misunderstandings, making readers think that Webpack will do the injection job？
author: knockkk

body:
Hi @alexander-akait - I am using the [HTML Webpack Plugin](https://github.com/jantimon/html-webpack-plugin), however, I handle the injection manually using an HTML `.ejs` template as opposed to using `inject: true`.

Upon further inspection, I think I have misunderstood the documentation here. From what I now understand, the magic comments will provide Webpack context for how to inject the the `script` tags into the DOM upon evaluation, NOT compilation. The `scripts` inject upon compilation are the 'entry/initial' scripts, and then when the JS is evaluated, it will load any additional modules needed as and when required. For example, navigating to a new route, the JS will inject a new script tag into the DOM to load the module(s) necessary to run the route. The purpose of the magic comments is to advise Webpack whether to preload/prefetch these modules at entry point based on the likelihood they will be needed. Given modules cannot be 'prefetched', they can only be preloaded using `modulepreload`, the magic comments do not really apply to them...

So, with that in mind, I think this is more of a feature request than a bug report. This is how I would expect it to work:

**See the following:**

When a compilation is set to `target: ['es6']`, we handle the magic comments slightly differently:

This:
```
import(/* webpackPreload: true */ 'ChartingLibrary');
```

Will yield and injection of the following into the DOM:
```
<link rel="modulepreload" href="http://example.com/js/123.456789.chunk.js">
```

However, this:
```
import(/* webpackPrefetch: true */ 'ChartingLibrary');
```

Will either be ignored, or will yield the same as the above (I can't decide) - this will make this functionality backward compatible if people upgrade to ES6.
author: sinemacula-ben

Any import syntax today is built of plugins in webpack's source code. Example:

author: dhdaines

body:
Do you want to ignore only `console.log(new URL("./", import.meta.url).href);` or all `new URL(...)` syntax?

> Ideally it should also pass through unchanged any other instance of new URL(..., import.meta.url) where the target can't be resolved as an asset, since the user might create them after the fact.

can you clarify, we already do it, or Am I missing something?
author: alexander-akait

body:
> Do you want to ignore only `console.log(new URL("./", import.meta.url).href);` or all `new URL(...)` syntax?
> 
> > Ideally it should also pass through unchanged any other instance of new URL(..., import.meta.url) where the target can't be resolved as an asset, since the user might create them after the fact.
> 
> can you clarify, we already do it, or Am I missing something?

Hi! As mentioned above, webpack should make `new URL(relative_path, import.meta.url)` do what you expect it to do, that is, return a URL to the thing referenced by `relative_path`.  Currently, this works with assets that you want to bundle, see: https://webpack.js.org/guides/asset-modules/

The current behaviour of Webpack is *fantastic* and *fabulous* in the case where `relative_path` points to an asset that you want to bundle, i.e. a file relative to the source file containing `new URL`.  Webpack will bundle it, and rewrite the code so that you get a URL to the output asset.

The main problem is when it points to a directory.  Webpack will try to resolve it as a module.  This is wrong, because `new URL` is not a module import.  Minimally it should just pass it through in this case.

I think it would be helpful if it would also pass through anything else that it is unable to resolve to an asset, but maybe that gets into something beyond my comprehension of how Webpack works...
author: dhdaines

body: See specifically https://webpack.js.org/guides/asset-modules/#url-assets for the current (good) behaviour of webpack for files referenced with `new URL`

body:
Something wrong with `needBuild` in `NormalModule.js`:
1. First run we have `true` and `true` for `index.js` and `data.txt`
2. Second run the same
3. Third and etc only `true` for `index.js`

Update: here https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/eed37e93c5ea2eb18dd452953b6be33f0d5150b8/lib/FileSystemInfo.js#L2696

author: modjke

body:
For future fixes - the problem here:
https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/FileSystemInfo.js#L1201 for files
https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/FileSystemInfo.js#L1212 for directories

Shortly - watchpack returns `{}` for some files and directories (https://github.com/webpack/watchpack/blob/main/lib/DirectoryWatcher.js#L721 ) and return only `{ safeTime: 1682648759183 }` (directories), so we don't have the `timestampHash` properties for snapshots

So webpack thinks they are invalid when check files and directories and rebuild them on the second run and rewrite values on valid, and no problems on the third run.

The same for files, not only directories
author: alexander-akait

body:
`{}` seem to be the "existance only" object. So watchpack doesn't known about anything but that this file/directory exists.

That doesn't seem to be enough for snapshoting, so webpack need to read more information about this file/directory when snapshoting. The snapshoting already have an implementation for that in the case of `undefined`, but that doesn't seem to be used when `{}` is found. This should be fixed.

I am publishing a library and I cannot (prefer not to) ask everyone to configure webpack to manually create chunks. Transparent bundler support was one of main goals for `sqlite-wasm-http`.

I settled on using dynamic `import()` both in main and in the worker which works as expected and doesn't break Node.js.

The information in this issue is not valid anymore - this was about webpack 4 - webpack 5 is fully capable of splitting code between main and a worker - it simply doesn't recognize it in this case - just look at the fact that a slight change fixes it.

body:
Everything works as expected:

> node-fetch has "sideEffects": false in its package.json, and

You have `import * as __WEBPACK_EXTERNAL_MODULE_node_fetch_e2fb16d6__ from "node-fetch";` on the top, because it is external and `sideEffects` will not work in such case
author: alexander-akait
alexander-akait

body: You need `externalsType` due this https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/16181 we will fix it in future
author: alexander-akait

body:
> Everything works as expected…

Not yet – not for me anyway. The [Webpack docs](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/examples/side-effects/README.md) have this to say about `sideEffects`:

> The "sideEffects": false flag in big-module's package.json indicates that the package's modules have no side effects (on evaluation) and only expose exports. This allows tools like webpack to optimize re-exports.

I don't think it's controversial to interpret that flag as an indicator that – absent a use – an import can be reaped. If `node-fetch` was not marked as `"sideEffects": false` and Webpack left this code alone:

```ts
require('node-fetch');
```

…that would be understandable! We can't know that the _module factory_ of `node-fetch` is side-effect free (eg. it _might_ overwrite `globalThis.fetch`) so we _have_ to leave it alone, to be safe.

That's not what's going on here. `node-fetch` is marked as side-effect free, it's not used anywhere in the body of the compiled `index.ts`, but despite all of that, Webpack doesn't reap it.

Why?
author: steveluscher
steveluscher

body:
> node-fetch is marked as side-effect free
> That's not what's going on here. node-fetch is marked as side-effect free, it's not used anywhere in the body of the compiled index.ts, but despite all of that, Webpack doesn't reap it.

It is not applied for externals, because we don't know what is `node-fetch`, it can be a package, it can be a variable in global scope, it can be anything in scope and can be loaded from `window`, `network` (using http/https) and etc, so we can't undestand it is `sideEffects` free, so it will not work and never worked




> That code exports a Promise for a function, rather than a function, so that will not work. Also, import("node-fetch") still appears in the built output, so it doesn't achieve the goal.

Try to run it again, maybe you missing something, all works perfectly locally, it remove `node-fetch`, but yes, it resturns promise, you can await it too when you need or using top level awaits and don't have it at all

body:
I think a lot of people using Angular + custom webpack with the Define plugin are going to run into this issue, since  `@angular-devkit/build-angular` [depends specifically on `webpack@5.76.1`](https://github.com/angular/angular-cli/blob/15.2.x/packages/angular_devkit/build_angular/package.json#L64). 

Solutions I'm aware of (none are really ideal):
- `npm install` an older version of wepback like 5.78 that stays "compatible" with 5.76
- `npm dedupe` to hopefully get just one version of webpack (will be 5.76.1 for the angular scenario above) - but also dedupes other node modules

I don't know the "correct" solution here, but if it affects enough users, maybe the most user-friendly solution is to to keep webpack 5 versions compatible (i.e. don't rename methods)?

body:
### Storybook 6

With Yarn, using resolutions is a decent workaround `"@storybook/builder-webpack5/webpack": "5.83.1",` , eg:

##### package.json
```json
"resolutions": {
  "@storybook/builder-webpack5/webpack": "5.83.1"
}
```

Replace `"5.83.1"` with your webpack version.

This may also be possible with the **npm `overrides` field**, but I haven't tested it.

It is expected, `core.autocrlf` is an option for this (and you should use it), many of OSS repos use `* text=auto`:
- https://github.com/facebook/react/blob/main/.gitattributes#L1
- https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/main/.gitattributes#L1

Can list more if you want, set `core.autocrlf` to `input`, your IDE should read `.editorconfig` (most of IDE can do it) and we have `end_of_line = lf`

Also we have tests with different line endiging (for different testing purposes), so forcing is not a good idea

body:
For what it's worth, I too ran into this issue when cloning the webpack repo, and having Prettier immediately complain about the carriage returns:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2230453/232832256-ec6848a7-e108-45af-8c29-ac373b5efd87.png)

It was not obvious to me how to fix it (and fiddling with git settings didn't seem to help, though I'll admit it was my first time encountering something like this, so I may have missed the obvious). I eventually did find a solution, albeit a somewhat awkward one:

> 0. If `node_modules` is already created, delete it (e.g., `rimraf node_modules`). That will make the script run much faster.
> 1. Open the folder in Windows Explorer
> 2. Right click and open Git Bash
> 3. Run `find . -name "*.js" -exec dos2unix {} \;`
> 4. Commit the resulting file changes in my clone/branch/fork. (It's OK, they don't show up in the eventual PR into Webpack).

(Steps inspired by https://superuser.com/a/1252691)

It took several minutes for the script to scan through all the files, but it did do the trick once it was done.

@TheLarkInn , would definitely be good to have the definitive steps in the contributing guide, and hopefully simpler ones than what I ended up doing :-). And ideally, to have things "just work" out of the box if that's feasible.

author: ArcanoxDragon

body:
I have discovered some quirky behavior while trying to work around this issue in my company's Webpack project. It seems the _ordering_ of the keys in the `entry` object given in the Webpack config can change the output, specifically the module IDs.

We derive our entry points dynamically at compile time. Our project structure is something like the following:

```
webpack.config.ts
projects/
    a/
        scripts/
            entry-point-a.ts
        src/
            moduleOne.ts
        styles/
            _partial-a.scss
            stylesheet-a.scss
    b/
        scripts/
            entry-point-b.ts
        src/
            moduleTwo.ts
        styles/
            _partial-b.scss
            stylesheet-b.scss
```

The files in each project's `scripts` and `styles` folders are discovered as entry points using [`globby`](/sindresorhus/globby). We turn the array of files returned by `globby` into a map of entry points to pass to the `entry` option on our Webpack config. The entry object looks something like this:

```js
{
    "a/entry-point-a": "./projects/a/scripts/entry-point-a.ts",
    "b/entry-point-b": "./projects/b/scripts/entry-point-b.ts",
    "styles/a/stylesheet-a": "./projects/a/styles/stylesheet-a.scss",
    "styles/b/stylesheet-b": "./projects/b/styles/stylesheet-b.scss",
}
```

I have been struggling to reproduce this "non-deterministic build" problem locally, but it occurs quite consistently on our automated build server. Purely on a random hunch, I decided to try _shuffling_ the ordering of the keys in the `entry` object I give to Webpack (to simulate if `globby` gave us files back in a different order than last time). Sure enough, the output changed completely. Certain things that were mangled became non-mangled and vice versa, and the numeric module IDs got shuffled around. Every single build I ran with shuffled input was different.

I then took out the shuffling code and instead did a `.sort()` on the array I get back from `globby`. The build output is back to being deterministic locally, though I have not tried this fix on our build server yet.

@nanramakr: does your Webpack config also use dynamically-derived entry points? What happens if you try sorting the entry object keys in a deterministic order?
author: ArcanoxDragon

> @jessevanassen as a work around can you get by this by dynamically importing an esm module which `import` the json instead? We're still looking into the fix for this.

As a workaround, we rewrote it to the following which also works, but doesn't require a separate file:

```js
const json = (await import("./file.json")).default;
```

It's purely the combination of dynamic imports and destructuring that triggers it, by taking out the destructuring the bug doesn't occur.

body:
This RFC Work should be broken down into (at least) two pieces: 
- [ ] Restructure Data Format to accomplish 
  - [ ] Add additional data structures in Stats
  - [ ] Additional Profiling API's can be stored in https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/ModuleProfile.js
- [ ] Creation of the UI/Reporting (Plugin)

Should we have a format for Vite/Parcel/webpack/rspack/esbuild/etc/Prior Work
- https://bundle-buddy.com
- https://github.com/relative-ci/bundle-stats
- https://github.com/statoscope/statoscope
author: TheLarkInn

body: another motivate for structural metainfo is that when we migrate some buildtools to anther buildtools | version, we want to know which has changed, since diff the bundle assets code is nearly not readable for human, we always do stats diff | meta diff to find out whether module|assets|chunk|resolveResult changed | deleted | added.

It is by design, because `3.0.0-dev.` is not greater than `3.0.0`, for example, before release you can use `-alpha`/`-beta` (and any other prerelease tags), and they are less then `3.0.0`, you can find more infromation here https://github.com/npm/node-semver#prerelease-tags

> ^1.2.3-beta.2 := >=1.2.3-beta.2 <2.0.0-0 Note that prereleases in the 1.2.3 version will be allowed, if they are greater than or equal to beta.2. So, 1.2.3-beta.4 would be allowed, but 1.2.4-beta.2 would not, because it is a prerelease of a different [major, minor, patch] tuple.

body:
Expected, run your code without `webpack` (i.e. `node ./entry-point.js`) and you will have the same behaviour, also you need to use `".": "./main.js"`, and also I strongly recommend to use `new URL("path/to/file.ext", import.meta.url);` for loading "external code"/"files" and avoid using `import`, because it will allow to run your code without bundler (if you have vanilla JS) and will be more compatible between different enviroments and spec says other things when you import JS file.

Sorry, we can't change default Node.js logic, if you still think it is a wrong behaviour, please open an issue in Node.js repo

Also if you really need this, you can create a small plugin for the resolver to work with it.

But again, it is very unsafe because you include raw code in your bundle, I strongly recommend to use `new URL(...)` syntax and use `asset/resource`/`asset/inline` for your case.

author: alexander-akait

body:
Nothing is stopping webpack from separating `path` from `?url` _before_ passing it to the resolver. I don’t think I've ever seen anyone using search parameters in exports maps, so it does not belong there.

I made a [small repro](https://github.com/fregante/webpack-sandbox/tree/main/exports-plus-search-params) with webpack and vite. Build output and logs are committed too. It points to `p-memoize`, which effectively uses `"exports": "./index.js"`, the most restrictive type of exports map. Vite can handle `"p-memoize?raw"` and `"p-memoze?url"`
author: fregante

body:
> Nothing is stopping webpack from separating path from ?url before passing it to the resolver

No we can't, otherwise it will break `exports` logic and you will get access to private modules, we **should not** change default Node.js resolving behavior. This issue was previously discussed with the node team and other developers, and everyone jointly decided not to provide this ability because it could violate a lot of things

> Vite can handle "p-memoize?raw" and "p-memoze?url"

Vite uses `?raw` and `?url` query parameters to specify how to generate module output and that is why they are stripping from original request, we don't provide such ability and pass all query parameters to original resource, technically vite violates resolving spec too here

The specification clearly states how resolving should work for the `export` field, and we do not want to give the opportunity to violate the specification
author: alexander-akait

body:
> you will get access to private modules

If you can resolve `"p-memoize"`, I'm only asking to resolve `"p-memoize?raw"` the same way as `"p-memoize"` _and then_ treat it as an asset. This has nothing to do resolving files not listed by the `exports` map.



> otherwise it will break `exports` logic

The only reason I see here, I just found out, is that search parameters _can_ be part of the exports map. 

```json
{
  "name": "my-lib",
  "exports": {
    ".": "./root.js",
    "./?param": "./param.js"
  }
}
```

and this works in node:

```js
import('my-lib');        // loads root.js
import('my-lib/?param'); // loads param.js
```

This satisfies me 👍
author: fregante

body:
> The only reason I see here, I just found out, is that search parameters can be part of the exports map.

Yes, they can be part and can be used how you write above

> If you can resolve "p-memoize", I'm only asking to resolve "p-memoize?raw" the same way as "p-memoize" and then treat it as an asset. This has nothing to do resolving files not listed by the exports map.

Please read the resolving specification (on Node.js docs site). You can return different modules using query parameters (as you done above). We can't simply allow `?raw`, since someone could export `?raw` and return another module (I know that more likely no one will do it, but this is a clear description of the logic how resolving should work), this would break the resolving logic at all, that is how resolving works.

author: TheLarkInn

body:
hm.. not sure what to suggest. It affects parser api in sensitive part, if we would like to change this. So basically what webpack does here:

lets give an example `a["b"].c["d"]`

For each chain member => if it is computed `["balbla"]` leads to `computed: true` => webpack tries to evaluate member ( `parser.evaluateExpression(member)` => if result is `BasicEvaluateExpresion` ( dont remember correct class name ) =>
webpack check `evaluated.asString()` and if it is a string => continue loop

If all members are static analyzable by webpack ( evaluated or computed=false ) it calls `callMemberChain` ( dont remember correct hook name ) hook, with members list as `string[]` ( first of all it will try to call `callExpression` `a.b.c.d`, I guess )

```
callMemberChain.call("a.b.c", ["d"]);
callMemberChain.call("a.b", ["c", "d"])
callMemberChain.call(importTag, ["b", "c", "d"])
```

So what you need is to **change hook api** :
- add 3rd param there 
- handle this `isComputed` thing in all members hooks in parser ( I guess it is in one place )
- pass additional info in dependency
- render in template in a way it was defined

Another option:
- improve Google Closure Compiler to handle this =)

------------------------------

However, I have a question why it is an issue? with mangling enabled in webpack all chain members that are exports will be mangled, if member is a part of module object, like in your example, will it even work on module level? maybe it works when modules are concatenated?
author: vankop

body:
RE: why is this an issue.  Mangling just mangles the imported object and not any chaining thereafter.  Which is good, because it doesn't rewrite or control the _other_ side of the code where the chained objects are created so mangling any further would break code at runtime.  RE: concatenation. We can't rely on concatenation as (a) it is an option the user can choose to disable, and (b) there are plenty of cases that cause concatenation bail-out even when it is enabled.

Webpack 4 was able to work with the Closure Compiler for excellent minification.  Webpack 5 has broken that, and if our team is to move to Webpack 5 then we need to get this functionality restored.

The core issue is that the chain elements following `obj1` are not the responsibility of Webpack, and should not be rewritten.

Webpack doesn't even consistently rewrite elements.  In the case of [optional chaining](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Operators/Optional_chaining), Webpack currently bails out and stops rewriting the expression at that point.  My guess is that this was done because the goal was to rewrite _at least_ the imported object reference, and it is easy to detect and use this optional point because it is guaranteed that there are no nested module or imported objects referenced in an optional way.  

What I'm looking for is to have Webpack determine the actual point of the imported object in the expression and stop rewriting there so it _at most_ rewrites the imported object reference.

I don't think that `isComputed` is sufficient because the module namespaces and imported objects always need to be rewritten in dot notation to match the generated export code.  So we still need to know where in the chained expression the imported object stops and its first member access starts.
author: bworline
vankop

body:
> What I'm looking for is to have Webpack determine the actual point of the imported object

there is no way to do this on module level, thats why webpack need to preserve all computed=true and then use it for non-exports members in chain
author: vankop

body:
maybe there is another option when 3rd argument ( probably it is 4th since full expression always also provided )  in hook is where expression range of `N`s member ended e.g.:
```js
callMemberChain.call(importTag, currentExpression, ["b", "c", "d"], fullExpression, [10, 12, 14])
```
so in dependency webpack will have `range` ( expression range ), where `range[0]` will be `start` and one number of array in argument will be `end`. This way webpack can always rewrites only exports part @TheLarkInn
author: vankop

body: Ok, so that will give all of the possible ranges.  When rewriting the expression, how to best determine the exports part of an expression and pick the correct range?

Please use:
```
experiments: {
    futureDefaults: true,

  },

CSS is under experiments right now, also you can see warnings, because

The global namespace object is a Node.js feature and isn't available in browsers.

author: alexander-akait

body:
Other solution when you enable `futureDefault` is set `type: "javascript/auto",` for `CSS`, i.e.
```
{
        test: /\.css$/,
        sideEffects: true,
        type: "javascript/auto",
        include: [
         path.resolve(__dirname, 'src/style.css'),
        ],
        use: [
          MiniCssExtractPlugin.loader,
          'css-loader'
        ]
      },
```

I will improve warnings in `css-loader`

body: However, there is an inconsistency! When I switch to static federation (i.e. all the remotes are listed in the webpack configs of the two apps) if in app2 I use `import foo from app1/test` the module is indeed executed only once. However if I use `const foo = import('app1/test')` it is again executed twice. The inconsistency is that if I do the same test for a local module in the same two cases the local module gets executed only once. Shouldn't remote modules behave the same way as local modules?
author: alex-vukov

body:
> However if I use const foo = import('app1/test') it is again executed twice

It means you have racing, it is not good for an application

> Shouldn't remote modules behave the same way as local modules?

local modules !== remote modules, they don't have sync, because `remote` application has own state, `local` application has own state and as I said above there are side effects, anyway you can implement sync on the application side by hands

body:
@Iswaryasl 

> Can you please elaborate how to use it in runtime.

Use what? You just define own function and run `setPublicPath` when you need it.

Also note - `__webpack_public_path__` will not work for static assets, like CSS, HTML and etc, so dynamic overriding `__webpack_public_path__` should be the last solution if nothing was found. So I recommend reconsider your configuration

author: alexander-akait

body:
> it was dicussed a lot of time and you can found a lot of issues about it (in 2020, 2021, 2022)

This is not possible, because the TypeScript-specific file extensions I’m referring to were first created one year ago, in 2022.

> Developer can setup `type: "javascript/esm"` on the loader level

Can you say more about this? It sounds like what I’m proposing, but I couldn’t find anything about it in the docs. https://webpack.js.org/guides/ecma-script-modules/#flagging-modules-as-esm doesn’t give much info on how a loader would interact with this.
author: andrewbranch
alexander-akait

body: Yeah, that doesn’t look like something we can expect every user to set up in their own ts-loader config. Also, it doesn’t seem like it allows the `type` for a `.ts` file to be set based on its package.json context. User-land config may be an interim solution, but at some point we would expect webpack + [any TypeScript loader] to implement an interop behavior that is possible to type check without additional configuration.
author: andrewbranch

body:
@andrewbranch esbuild and webpack do the same:
```
esbuild --bundle ./src/entry.mjs &> esbuild.js && node esbuild.js
```

output:
```
from .js default
from .mjs { default: 'default' }
```

webpack

```
webpack-cli && node ./dist/main.js
```

output:
```
from .js default
from .mjs { default: 'default' }
```

I think without additional options on typescript level it is impossible to solve, look at other bundlers, they have the same problem and provide option(s) to change logic (some of them are broken by default :smile: ), so developers can setup any configuration and you will not work out of the box.

> Yeah, that doesn’t look like something we can expect every user to set up in their own ts-loader config. Also, it doesn’t seem like it allows the type for a .ts file to be set based on its package.json context. User-land config may be an interim solution, but at some point we would expect webpack + [any TypeScript loader] to implement an interop behavior that is possible to type check without additional configuration.

Webpack knows nothing about ts-loader (other loaders). So you can ask about it `ts-loader` developers. We internally discussed about changing it before release webpack v5, but when checking popular repositories at that time, it broke almost everything, now I think the situation is much better, but unfortunately still not good enough.

Technically you are right and I agree that `"type": "module"` should set `"javascript/esm"` everywhere, we can start warning about potential problems and then, in future, enable it by default (I think we have an issue about it)
author: alexander-akait

body:
> Technically you are right and I agree that `"type": "module"` should set `"javascript/esm"` everywhere

This is very much not what I’m proposing. What we need is for `.ts` files to act like `.js` files and `.mts` files to act like `.mjs` files. That’s all.

> I think without additional options on typescript level it is impossible to solve

I am the person trying to make additional options in TypeScript; that’s why I opened this issue 😄 Additional options are impossible for us to make for bundlers that can’t treat TS extensions the same as JS extensions.

> esbuild and webpack do the same

Please see https://andrewbranch.github.io/interop-test/#synthesizing-default-exports-for-cjs-modules again—esbuild differs between `.mjs` and `.js` files like Webpack; I think this is good 👍. But esbuild also treats `.mts` and `.mjs` files the same (good 👍), where Webpack treats them differently (bad 👎). That’s why esbuild gets the 💙 in the table and Webpack does not. My goal here is to get Webpack a 💙.
author: andrewbranch

body:
> Please see https://andrewbranch.github.io/interop-test/#synthesizing-default-exports-for-cjs-modules again—esbuild differs between .mjs and .js files like Webpack; I think this is good +1. But esbuild also treats .mts and .mjs files the same (good +1), where Webpack treats them differently (bad -1). That’s why esbuild gets the blue_heart in the table and Webpack does not. My goal here is to get Webpack a blue_heart.

Hm, can you provide an example with webpack configuration and code? I may be confused because it should work identically, if not, I want to say it is a bug on ts-loader side, but I want to investigate
author: alexander-akait

body: And what output do you expect, just to clarify
author: alexander-akait

body:
https://github.com/andrewbranch/example-webpack-ts-extensions

I would be thrilled if it were a ts-loader bug, but I didn’t think loaders had a way of controlling this.
author: andrewbranch

body:
> I would be thrilled if it were a ts-loader bug, but I didn’t think loaders had a way of controlling this.

Thank you, I will look soon, webpack doesn't know when source code is stricky ESM, so I am afraid we can't do this without loader API, `mts` and `cts`, and even `ts` are unknown extensions :smile: 

But I will provide more feedback later
author: alexander-akait

body:
@andrewbranch I looked at code and yeah, `type: "javascript/esm"` solves the problem, technically it can be fixed here https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/config/defaults.js#L633, just change it to `test: /\.m(js|ts)$/i,`, **but**:
- webpack doesn't know non standard extensions and I think loader should do this things 
- Is it possible to generate `commonjs`/other in `mts`? Is it **always** ESM? I don't know some small details so need to clarify
- Another very simple solution is put `loaderContext._module.type = "javascript/esm";` in ts-loader, here https://github.com/TypeStrong/ts-loader/blob/main/src/index.ts#L136 (it will not work for `happypack`, but it is not popular usage, for happypack users the solution is set `type: "javascript/esm"` manually)

I think the last solution is better because only ts-loader and typescript know when the source code is ESM (I think typescript can have API to get it, at the least there is `tsconfig.json` to undestand it). 

Also I can implement public API to change `type` of a module (but it will require some dicussions some loaders may start to abuse it and this is not very good, although it is still possible) to avoid using `_module`.

And yes - updating ts-loader docs for ESM will be great too. 

Also my concern is that if we add `cts` and `mts`, other tools can start to ask do add them extensions too, this will make many settings inflexible and may create a situation when, with a new update of a tool we will need to change something, and this may be a breaking change(s).
author: alexander-akait

body:
I agree that a loader would be a good place to set the module kind. It’s great that there’s a (private API?) way to do it already!

> Is it possible to generate commonjs/other in mts?

Only under unsupported combinations of tsconfig options. I’m looking into making those combinations illegal.

> Also I can implement public API to change `type` of a module (but it will require some dicussions some loaders may start to abuse it and this is not very good, although it is still possible) to avoid using `_module`.

I think this would be great. Please keep me posted on these discussions. Thanks for looking into this!
author: andrewbranch

body:
> Only under unsupported combinations of tsconfig options. I’m looking into making those combinations illegal.

Yeah, it will be good 

> I think this would be great. Please keep me posted on these discussions. Thanks for looking into this!

Theoretically, we can fix it right now :smile: Also if we will use `loaderContext._module.type = "javascript/esm";` we will support old webpack versions too, then we can implement public API to change it, so almost every webpack versions (for 5) will work with it
author: alexander-akait

body: Yeah. I’ll plan to open a PR on ts-loader (maybe some others?), but a public API would be good, just so I can be sure the functionality will be available in future Webpack versions.

body: @andrewbranch Just for information - there is an issue https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/17288 and it provides a good example why this issue is still make sense, briefly - when you set `javascript/esm` webpack makes more optimizations, under the hood we have the `isStrictHarmony` property, it means module doesn't mixed `require`/`import` (and mixed commonjs/esm stuff) and so we can make more aggressive optimizations

author: chenxinyanc

body:
Okay I've updated the repo. Please check it out.

There is a line in root `package.json` making the whole package using ESM
```
  "type": "module"
```

`import default` doesn't unwrap the UMD module and behaves exactly the same as `import * as ...`

![image](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/assets/50703224/4141b432-a6ec-46e6-858d-a6c7f63a84cc)

As long as I remove `type: "module"` from `package.json`, everything works as expected -- I can consume UMD module even if I'm using ES6 import syntax (instead of `require("...")`).

![image](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/assets/50703224/816fe770-5553-4fb3-8971-113fa26ca687)

Here is the [entry point of `highcharts-react-official` package](https://github.com/highcharts/highcharts-react/blob/master/src/HighchartsReact.js), just for your reference.
author: chenxinyanc

author: alexander-akait
alexander-akait

body:
Thanks for your detailed explanation! I think I've been having misunderstanding regarding to this statement for a while:

> And don't know who written docs about that you can use default import in pure ESM, it is not truth.

After some googling, I found [this article](https://adamcoster.com/blog/commonjs-and-esm-importexport-compatibility-examples) & nodejs/node#40891 and realized that NodeJS (and therefore other bundlers) is not following the `__esModule` sentry (proposed by TypeScript, I guess).

So, in summary, I think the current situation is, when importing CJS modules,
* Default imports in NodeJS (and webpack / esbuild / etc.) behaves exactly the same as star imports.
* There is no way to overwrite default import unless the module imported is also ESM, rather than CJS.

Does it sound correct to you?
author: chenxinyanc

body:
> Default imports in NodeJS (and webpack / esbuild / etc.) behaves exactly the same as star imports.

Yes

> There is no way to overwrite default import unless the module imported is also ESM, rather than CJS.

Yes, but you can have `babel`/`swc`/etc to override/change it
author: alexander-akait

I've reproduced the issue by swapping the terser dep using yarn's `resolutions` thing.

However I found that the [TERSER_DEBUG_DIR](https://terser.org/docs/reporting-issues/#obtaining-the-source-code-given-to-terser) env var doesn't get passed down to Terser by the benchmark (or webpack) so I'm not sure how I can grab the inputs.
author: fabiosantoscode

body:
@fabiosantoscode 

> I've reproduced the issue by swapping the terser dep using yarn's resolutions thing.

Great

> However I found that the [TERSER_DEBUG_DIR](https://terser.org/docs/reporting-issues/#obtaining-the-source-code-given-to-terser) env var doesn't get passed down to Terser by the benchmark (or webpack) so I'm not sure how I can grab the inputs.

We use https://github.com/webpack-contrib/terser-webpack-plugin to run terser and we use multithreading, that is why `[TERSER_DEBUG_DIR](https://terser.org/docs/reporting-issues/#obtaining-the-source-code-given-to-terser)` is not avaliable, just do moneky patch https://github.com/webpack-contrib/terser-webpack-plugin/blob/master/src/index.js#L344 and return `0` 

I will look late how we can fix it inside plugin and pass `TERSER_DEBUG_DIR`
author: alexander-akait

body:
I was able to grab the input through changing the webpack.config.js of the common-libs folder to disable `optimization.minimize` and grabbing dist/main.js.

The suspicious commit https://github.com/terser/terser/commit/21600d91cf6dd071b45e76922b15975f5dcf879f slows down the `compress` step significantly compared to the commit before. This slowdown doesn't occur when `reduce_vars` is disabled.

Before, it took 8.1s, at the bad commit it takes 23.0s

body:
**tl;dr**: This seems to be a regression in NodeJS that was introduced in NodeJS 16.11 after they upgraded the V8 engine, it's still an issue.

**Details**:

We've been running into memory issues in our NextJS build (latest v12) and some of our Jest test suites. The common factor between the build process and the test suites are the presence of dynamic imports in the code. The test suites that don't test code with dynamic imports, don't seem to have memory issues, the ones that do, do. And the code we're building in our NextJS build process contains a number of dynamic imports.

We eventually noticed the following Jest issue that's currently still open https://github.com/jestjs/jest/issues/11956 and the fix mentioned there (`--workerIdleMemoryLimit` jest argument) seemed to improve things for us on the Jest side of things. As for NextJS we're currently attempting to resolve this by tweaking the `--max-old-space-size` we pass to node via `NODE_OPTIONS` and making sure we try to calculate it based on the maximum amount of memory and cpu resources we're making available during the build process, as the NODE_OPTIONS in our case get distributed to a number of NodeJS processes that NextJS seems to spawn (could also be Webpack doing that under the hood, we didn't dig too too deeply into that side of things).

Would it be possible for Webpack to implement a similar option until the issue in NodeJS gets resolved for good?

body:
Hi @TheLarkInn  I am interested in solving this bug, but I would like to get some help if it is possible.
Currently, I found that when `ConcatenatedModule` generates code, it would call `NormalModule` to generate code, and NormalModule would generate code by Template, In this case, `HarmonyImportSpecifierDependencyTemplate` would be used to replace some variable identifier to `WEBPACK_REFERENCE` identifier.
https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/853bfda35a0080605c09e1bdeb0103bcb9367a10/lib/dependencies/HarmonyImportSpecifierDependency.js#L320
I think there are two solutions that are possible.
The first is to make all variables that referencece to import variable pass this condition below, which would make the template replace it.
https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/853bfda35a0080605c09e1bdeb0103bcb9367a10/lib/dependencies/HarmonyImportSpecifierDependency.js#L325
The second is traversal AST to find that identifier reference to import variables and replace it.
I think the first one is more possible. I would like to hear your advice ~ thanks

author: fabiosantoscode

body:
Looks like something is passing the `ecma: 2015` option, which makes Terser assume the recepient of its code can cope with these unescaped object keys.

I think this is a remnant of the time where setting `ecma` was necessary for the Terser parser to accept modern syntax, which is no longer the case. It now accepts any JS, however modern and the `ecma` option only changes the compression and output steps.

If it's necessary to support old Terser and new Terser simultaneously, `parse.ecma` can be set instead of the root `ecma`, so as to only apply to the parse step.

The failure in the screenshot with `À€` underlined probably was a Terser issue at some point, but it works now. I modified remove-accents to add the EUR sign somewhere and the output is correct.

```js
/*...*/={"À€":"A",Á:"A",Â:"A",/*...*/
```

Notice how only the problematic `À€` is quoted.

body:
Hello everyone, what's progress on this?

I see some activity related to that issue:
- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/19891
- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/20347
- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/20353

## The current state

I just faced with the same problem on my project.

I use latest webpack version, I have webpack config that build `target: 'web'`.

My `.ts` file have code

```ts
new Worker(
    /* webpackChunkName: "NotesImporter.worker" */ new URL(
        './NotesImporter.worker',
        import.meta.url,
    ),
    { type: 'module' },
),
```

The worker is a `.ts` file that have import `remark-stringify` that is eventually import `decode-named-character-reference` and its package.json [contains](https://github.com/wooorm/decode-named-character-reference/blob/ade22666b2e3dceee6a4dc2b73293c441292904a/package.json#L21-L29) the following

```json
  "exports": {
    "deno": "./index.js",
    "edge-light": "./index.js",
    "react-native": "./index.js",
    "worker": "./index.js",
    "workerd": "./index.js",
    "browser": "./index.dom.js",
    "default": "./index.js"
  },
```

In our webpack config we have

```
	resolve: {
		byDependency: {
			worker: {
				conditionNames: ['worker', 'import', 'module', 'default'],
			},
		},
	},
```

so **my expectations is**:
- Code for main thread builds well
- WebWorker `NotesImporter.worker.ts` will be resolved via standard webpack rules
- Any imports in file `NotesImporter.worker.ts` or its transitive imports will use config `conditionNames: ['worker', 'import', 'module', 'default']` so package `decode-named-character-reference` will be resolved as `decode-named-character-reference/index.js`

The **actual behavior** is:
- Code for main thread builds well
- The worker code throws error `ReferenceError: document is not defined`

I've also tried update webpack config to

```
	resolve: {
		byDependency: {
			esm: {
                conditionNames: ['worker', 'import', 'module', 'browser'],
			},
		},
	},
```

It completely break our app:
- No errors in runtime
- Worker loads fine
- **The UI of our app looks broken**. Probaby it's because UI frameworks load incorrect bundle.

## The question

I just want to know about progress on this problem.

If I miss something or you think I understand something wrong, please do correct me.

author: denzelem

body:
From what I understand about the webpack source code, this is an expected behavior, layer is just a modifier prepend to the module's identifier, which can make the module have different instance with different layer.

In this reproduce, if you want to only have one instance of `large_lib.js`, you can specific the `large_lib.js`'s layer to `common`

```js
module: {
  rules: [
    { test: /large_lib\.js/, layer: 'common' }
  ]
}
```

before:

`(layer dark) dark.js` -> `(layer dark) main.js` -> `(layer dark) large_lib.js`
`(layer light) light.js` -> `(layer light) main.js` -> `(layer light) large_lib.js`

after:

`(layer dark) dark.js` -> `(layer dark) main.js` -> `(layer common) large_lib.js`
`(layer light) light.js` -> `(layer light) main.js` --^

body:
Hi, @alexander-akait  @maulshh  It seems like the function webpack generated have really using object mapping as a lookup table, 
https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/1f99ad6367f2b8a6ef17cce0e058f7a67fb7db18/lib/runtime/GetChunkFilenameRuntimeModule.js#L285
I guess when the webpack generates code, it would make a sequent of if statements into ternary operation, unfortunately, i do not familiar with webpack source code and arch, could you help me find out where code generation logic is ? thanks ~
author: Steven19Lee

body: this bug is actually caused by Terser minification rather than webpack. > have really using object mapping as a lookup That `Array` is for multiple ids that resolve to the same chunk-name, and it will return an if statement https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/1f99ad6367f2b8a6ef17cce0e058f7a67fb7db18/lib/runtime/GetChunkFilenameRuntimeModule.js#L289 it will create a chain of if statements and terser minification will change it to nested ternary causing the stackoverflow error.

author: IdeaHunter

body:
I've got the same issue with an HTML fragment (which contains styles and scripts of pure CSS entry) loaded (with jQuery) in the document. The script try to find the CSS module, but can't find any because the stylesheet it's not loaded yet (non blocking?).

I think it's related to [how entry startup work](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/1f13ff9fe587e094df59d660b4611b1bd19aed4c/lib/javascript/StartupHelpers.js#L51-L55) that consider the stylesheet not a chunk, means all modules should be available synchronously when the entry start.
Maybe the [CSS loading runtime](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/1f13ff9fe587e094df59d660b4611b1bd19aed4c/lib/css/CssLoadingRuntimeModule.js#L171) should wait/watch each stylesheet loading to detect if the CSS module is loaded (via the custom CSS variable). 
Or [use additional attributes](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/16147#issuecomment-1596316526) that describe the file content (and optionally need to detect if the file load or fail to load)
