author: hardfist

body:
@hardfist 

> it is loaded but finish loaded after js start execute，there is no mechanism on js side that waiting until css is fully loaded before js start execute

Weird, because `link` mandatory for loading according to specification...

Can you demonstrate the problem?
author: alexander-akait
hardfist

body: @hardfist Oh `safari browser`...
author: alexander-akait

body:
> @hardfist
> 
> > it is loaded but finish loaded after js start execute，there is no mechanism on js side that waiting until css is fully loaded before js start execute
> 
> Weird, because `link` mandatory for loading according to specification...
> 
> Can you demonstrate the problem?

As [DOMContentLoaded page on MDN](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Document/DOMContentLoaded_event) mention it:
> `DOMContentLoaded` does not wait for style sheets to load, however deferred scripts do wait for style sheets, and the `DOMContentLoaded` event is queued after deferred scripts.

But.. it's only the case if the style sheet is created directly by the document parser.
That means, [in my case](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/17611#issuecomment-1738842049), the JS that inject the HTML fragments must load style sheets before it inject scripts to simulate script-blocking style sheet.

See also [4.2.7 Interactions of styling and scripting - HTML Standard](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/semantics.html#interactions-of-styling-and-scripting)
author: mems

body:
@mems 

> As [DOMContentLoaded page on MDN](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Document/DOMContentLoaded_event) mention it:

> DOMContentLoaded does not wait for style sheets to load, however deferred scripts do wait for style sheets, and the DOMContentLoaded event is queued after deferred scripts.

We don't use `DOMContentLoaded`, we use `load` event and wait it

> But.. it's only the case if the style sheet is created directly by the document parser.
> That means, https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/17611#issuecomment-1738842049, the JS that inject the HTML fragments must load style sheets before it inject scripts to simulate script-blocking style sheet.

I think something like this should be organized by the app
author: alexander-akait

body: I reproduced and am looking deeper
author: alexander-akait

body: The problem is here - `getComputedStyle`, because Safari fires DomReady before linked resources and deferred scripts are loaded... And unlocks the universal target, CSS is last to finis it
author: alexander-akait

body: Prioritized the problem, in the next release we will fix it, this will open the possibility to use it in old browsers too

body: For context I discovered this bailout because we're using CSS modules which export something like `{ "source-class-name": "a9-c" }`. Due to the order Webpack writes concatenated modules we aren't actually shaking out the lengthy `"source-class-name"` key names. We can work around it on our end by using lambda expressions which compress better anyway but I believe a fix for the underlying issue would provide byte savings for most applications.

body:
Revisiting this: the deopt no longer reproduces with current tooling.

The original report relied on Terser being **order-dependent** in module scope — folding `payload.key` only when the `const` was emitted before the escaping function. Terser 5.48 (and swc) are now order-**independent** there: they constant-fold the property read and drop the object entirely regardless of where webpack installs the export getter.

I measured the reported scenario (hoisted `export function` closing over a multi-key object, module isolated in its own chunk so the export escapes with no visible caller) across four minifiers, both concatenated and non-concatenated, at `passes: 1` and `passes: 2`:

| Minifier | constant values | non-constant values | reorder unlocks DCE? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Terser 5.48 | folds object away (both orders) | keeps object (both) | no |
| swc | folds object away (both orders) | keeps object (both) | no |
| esbuild | keeps object (both orders) | keeps object (both) | no |
| uglify-js | keeps object (both orders) | keeps object (both) | no |

Real webpack production builds of the reported repro are byte-identical whether the export getter is placed before or after the module body. Moving the getter changes output by at most one byte (the `d(...)` call's own position), never a dead-code-elimination win.

So the byte savings the report was after are already captured by the minifier. #21350 adds a regression test that locks in the optimized output for this shape, and closes this issue as resolved by tooling improvements. Happy to reopen if anyone still sees the keys survive on a current toolchain.

---
_Generated by [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/code)_

body: You seem to be generating the hash as a hexstring and then converting that to bytes. Surely you mean to generate the hash as bytes from the onset?
author: ErikLindroos

body:
Right now im simply trying to understand and mimic the internal way Webpack is doing this. The goal is to have a better understanding of how Webpack generates this so i can create a custom Hasher to match our ASP.NET MVC Bundling Urls that use cache-busting by adding a querystring param based on a hash of the file it sees when creating the bundle.

But i need a better understand of how Webpack is doing the hashing before i can even try to create a custom one. So i figured if i can get a Powershell version to match Webpack then i would have a better understanding of how its doing that to create my custom hasher.

MVC Basically does the following
1. Read the file to string
2. Converts string to bytes using `Encoding.Unicode.GetBytes()`
3. Hashes that to `SHA256`
4. To Base64 with URL support

body:
We've also hit this bug when trying to update Webpack, for us the failing code can be simplified to:
```
var AClass = class extends EventTarget {},
  anInstance = new AClass();
```
I did some regression testing:
5.89.0 fails
5.86.0 fails
5.85.0 fails
5.84.1 fails
5.84.0 works
5.83.1 works

I see  `5.84.1` had some inner graph changes, I guess that might be a suspect? as the workaround of setting `config.optimization.innerGraph = false;` seems to work.

body:
@alexander-akait  yep..

It's a huge project, server and client compiler will each compile 25k+ modules. I took some methods (from `next.js` and other repos) to optimize it.

1. only compile used route
2. shake unused modules earlier(in swc transfrom stage), e.g. remove `no_ssr` & `useEffect & useLayoutEffect` code in server compiler; use swc optimizier & minify options in swc-loader
3. optimize barrel packages

After above methods, server and client compiler will each compile 10k modules...(in MAC M1 Pro, from 240s startup to 110s) It looks like no more methods to make it less :(. 

I will try `rspack` to make compile fast in next few months

Do you know whether this is a regression @abirmingham?  (I'm planning to test v5.88.2 - the version I'm using for a work project at the moment - next to see whether the same behaviour occurs)
author: jayaddison

body: Yep, this was also replicable using v5.88.2 after three iterations - I haven't tested any earlier versions of `webpack`.
author: jayaddison

body: Hey James, thanks for taking a peek at this. I haven't tested any other versions with the minimal repro, but in my main project I am using `webpack@5.70.0` and am seeing issues. I hesitate to confirm that it is the same issue, however, as my main project is quite a bit more complex and the nondeterminism may be coming from other places. All of that is to say that I don't know if it is a regression or not :)
author: abirmingham

body:
You're welcome.  Issue #17009 sounds potentially related.

I notice that the [list of entrypoints generated in the webpack config](https://github.com/abirmingham/repro-webpack-issue-17757/blob/28d3e3a5783d37e5543a2d28a151e67d43caea59/webpack.config.js#L13-L16) seems deterministic (generated from numbers 0..6) - is there a reason to use that many input files?  The contents of each code file seem to be the same.
author: jayaddison

body: @jayaddison good question! While attempting to reproduce the issue, I noticed that projects under a certain size took a lot longer to reproduce the problem, or were unable to reproduce the problem at all. I wanted a short feedback loop, so I continued adding entrypoints until the problem was reliably reproduced in under 10 iterations. The same is true of the TS files in the `autogenerated` folder.
author: abirmingham

body:
Could you try removing more of the webpack config, JavaScript code in the modules, etc to continue to narrow this down without affecting the repeatability of it?  (I will try to do the same soon too, but not sure when I'll get around to it)

It'll probably be a slightly annoying process, but I think we should be able to continue to remove items until -- ideally -- there's a removal where we cross some threshold and the problem stops occurring.

Then there'll be the equally tricky part of debugging from there to determine why whatever small difference that is causes the problem in the first place.
author: jayaddison

body: Note: I briefly wondered whether [`realContentHash`](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/optimization/#optimizationrealcontenthash) could be relevant here; the documentation does mention that when it is set to `false`, content hashes may not be deterministic.  However: the [default for that setting is to match `production` flag](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/87660921808566ef3b8796f8df61bd79fc026108/lib/config/defaults.js#L1332) -- and that's set [`true` in the repro `webpack.config.js`](https://github.com/abirmingham/repro-webpack-issue-17757/blob/28d3e3a5783d37e5543a2d28a151e67d43caea59/webpack.config.js#L19).
author: jayaddison

body:
A few more observations:

* Removing the [`threadloader`](https://github.com/abirmingham/repro-webpack-issue-17757/blob/28d3e3a5783d37e5543a2d28a151e67d43caea59/webpack.config.js#L46) from the repro configuration seems to produce different results, something that's probably not ideal.
* From inspection of one of the differing-files using `cmp`, the difference tends to be in an object whose properties are integers and whose values look like hashes near the very end of the output `.js` files, just after a special-case `==='monaco'` check (maybe not relevant for the investigation, but a useful tracer: this may be due to some [`webpackChunkName` directives](https://github.com/abirmingham/repro-webpack-issue-17757/blob/28d3e3a5783d37e5543a2d28a151e67d43caea59/src/entrypoint_2.js#L27)).  Usually only one of the property values differs.
* It's possible to move most of the `repro.sh` contents into the `Dockerfile`, as long as the `webpack.config.js` and `src` directories are also `COPY`'d into the layers during build.  This is something I'm going to reduce size (albeit not really time, yet) of the iteration loop.
author: jayaddison

body:
I've confirmed that this variance is replicable as far back as `webpack=5.56.1`.  It may be replicable in earlier versions too, but that's the least-recent version I've tested so far (I was squinting at 3fa83c6c77de88b9874ecd06322ada787b90ac25 - but reproducing the issue on v5.56.1 predates that change, so I think it is unrelated).

So far it does seem that the [`thread-loader`](https://github.com/webpack-contrib/thread-loader) component makes this issue more repeatable.  Whether it is definitively the cause, however, I cannot yet confirm/reject.
author: jayaddison

body:
Hey @jayaddison - I was able to spend more time on this, and attempted the following changes:

#### 1
- Changes:
-- Dropped thread-loader to 2 workers, 2 workerParallelJobs
-- Dropped entrypoints from `Array(7)` to `Array(3)`
-- Load CPU: `for i in 1 2 3 4; do while : ; do : ; done & done`
- Results:
-- Reproduced on second build

#### 2
- Changes:
-- Dropped entrypoints from `Array(3)` to `Array(1)`
- Results:
-- Reproduced on 8th build
#### 3
- Changes:
-- Removed all extra entrypoints
- Results:
-- 13 builds failed to reproduce

#### 4
- Changes:
-- Reverted change # 3
-- Removed all references to `autogenerated` directory
- Results:
-- 50 builds failed to reproduce

#### 5
- Changes:
-- Reverted change #4
-- Removed half of autogenerated files
- Results:
-- Reproduced on 21st build

Here is the commit containing my stopping point, which can be seen in the repro repository on branch `10_23_2023_minimal_build`:
```
commit 21c561c1483963a1069943e5b351190faa990d89 (HEAD -> main, abirmingham-github/10_23_2023_minimal_build)
Author: Alex Birmingham <abirmingham@extrahop.com>
Date:   Mon Oct 23 15:39:51 2023 -0700

    Attempt More Minimal Reproduction
    
    - Delete half of autogenerated files
    - Reduce entrypoints from 8 to 2
    - Reduce thread-loader workers from 4 to 2
    - Reduce thread-loader workerParallelJobs from 3 to 
```

Note that in the final iteration the bug was only reproduced after the 21st build, and in a prior iteration I stopped running builds at 13 builds. So it is certainly possible that the bug would have been reproduced if builds had continued.

This is a tricky one. It's unclear to me whether the extra complexity is necessary or is simply helpful in reproducing the issue in a less prohibitive amount of time.

Thank you for your time!
author: abirmingham

body: Thanks - yep, more clues.  I do have a feeling that the `autogenerated` content -- and potentially the stack/module depth of it, the way that the modules are connected -- could be relevant.  And the entrypoints seem relevant, as you've confirmed.  At the moment I do think threading could be a relevant interaction too.  Perhaps it requires all three of those in combination, but we'll need more info to prove or disprove that.

@alexander-akait  Sorry, I have spent a lot of effort trying to reproduce, but failed because there is too much sensitive information and internal use of third party packages

I've tried turning off compression and this still happens frequently.

I can roughly describe my situation, I hope that helps.

1. There are a lot of third-party utils packages exporting fn1, fn2, etc. and these packages are configured with sideEffects:false.
 
2. There is a large number of pages that use fn1, fn2, etc. For example, pageA uses fn1, fn2, etc. For example, pageA uses fn1, fn2, fn3, PageB uses fn1, fn3, PageC uses fn2, fn3. Many similar scenarios.

3. the page is dynamically imported and loaded, so splitChunk is performed.

4. the output generates a large number of chunks and vendors. The presence of a large number of vendors is very similar. There are a lot of vendors that are very similar. The only difference in filenames is the contentHash.

5. The difference between the two packages is that the import vendors are not in the same order.
I've tried turning off compression and this still happens frequently.

body:
I investigated this issue using the reproduction repository:
[https://github.com/abirmingham/repro-webpack-issue-17757](https://github.com/abirmingham/repro-webpack-issue-17757?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

Results:

On Webpack 5.89.0, I was able to reproduce nondeterministic contenthash across builds.
The issue appeared most frequently when using thread-loader for parallel processing.
Changing other configuration options (e.g., optimization settings) did not eliminate the behavior.
After upgrading to Webpack 5.105.4, builds became deterministic.
Repeated builds (including with multiple thread-loader workers) no longer regenerated contenthash without input changes.

It appears the issue is no longer reproducible on recent Webpack versions.

Environment:
Node: v22.21.0
OS: Windows
author: Karan09542

body:
@Karan09542 Hi, thanks for the feedback. We actually fixed a deterministic chunkId issue in projects with circular dependencies in v5.104.3: webpack/webpack#20452, but we're not sure that this is related, and I haven’t been able to reproduce it, even with v5.89.0.

It would be very helpful if you could try an earlier version, such as v5.104.2, and let us know whether the issue still reproduces, appreciate it.
author: hai-x

body:
I investigated the behavior across newer Webpack versions starting from v5.104.1, where nondeterministic contenthash changes were reproducible.

On v5.104.1, the hashes frequently diverged when using thread-loader.
Adding CircularDependencyPlugin increased the divergence, although no circular dependencies were reported.

I also tested several additional factors:
- Disabled Terser parallelization (parallel: false)
- Changed the hashFunction algorithm
- Compared stats outputs between builds
- Verified file contents by removing [contenthash] from filenames

None of these changes resolved the issue on v5.104.1 — hashes still diverged while emitted file contents remained identical.

### Testing v5.105.0

Before testing, I performed a clean setup:
- Deleted node_modules
- Deleted package-lock.json
- Cleared npm cache (npm cache clean --force)
- Reinstalled dependencies

Using the same configurations as before:

Initially observed a small number of divergences between builds 391→400
(possibly due to filesystem artifacts, dirty output, or residual cache)
After rebuilding and repeating tests, no further divergences occurred
Subsequent runs remained stable across all configurations

### Testing v5.105.4 (latest)

Similar behavior was observed:
- One divergence occurred during the first runs
- After rebuilding and retesting, hashes remained stable
- No reproducible nondeterministic behavior was observed

```text
841->850->860->870->880 (with 4-thread-loader & 2-optimization & CircularDepsPlugin) // nothing with `no [contenthash], check if file content changes or not` also when add devtool: false | hidden-source-map | nosources-source-map
881->890 (with 4-thread-loader & 2-optimization & CircularDepsPlugin) // nothing happend when changing `hashFunction: xxhash64 to sha256`
--> 2 builds (with 4-thread-loader & 2-optimization & CircularDepsPlugin) // no change when comparing state1.json & state2.json
```

</details>
author: Karan09542

body:
Hi, I’ve been going through this issue and the recent discussion.

From what I understand, the nondeterministic contenthash behavior seems to have improved in newer Webpack versions (>=5.105.x), possibly related to fixes around chunkId determinism and circular dependencies.

Would it be helpful if I:
- Try reproducing this on older versions (e.g., 5.104.1 vs 5.105.x)
- Compare build outputs and stats
- Create a simplified reproduction case

Please let me know how I can best assist here.
author: pritam-31

body:
@hai-x I tested across versions as requested.

Deterministic ID strategies (based on stable module identifiers) alone are not sufficient to guarantee stable contenthash.

In earlier versions, module and chunk ordering depended on graph traversal, which was influenced by non-deterministic root selection in graphs without a clear root (e.g., cyclic/complex graphs). Since findGraphRoots relied on edge (insertion) ordering, traversal order could vary across builds.

The fix in PR #20452 (1313375f9) ensures deterministic root selection, which stabilizes traversal order. This results in consistent module and chunk ordering, and therefore stable contenthash.

This behavior reproduces in v5.104.1 and no longer reproduces in v5.105.2+.

Would appreciate confirmation that this matches the intended fix.
author: Karan09542

body:
Not sure if this is the same issue.  But I ran into ECONNREFUSED error trying to proxy normal http requests to localhost.  If I downgrade to node 16.20.1 then the proxying works again with localhost.

I did find out that starting with [node 17+, node prefers IPV6 over IPV4.](https://github.com/chimurai/http-proxy-middleware#nodejs-17-econnrefused-issue-with-ipv6-and-localhost-705)  So DNS resolution for localhost isn't guaranteed to be 127.0.0.1, it could be ::1.  When I used the ip address proxying worked again using node 17+.

body:
There are no problems with webpack itself, you ask typescript to use `require`, but try to load ESM module, please use:
```
{
    "compilerOptions": {
        "noImplicitAny": true,
        "target": "ES5",
        "moduleResolution": "bundler",
        "module": "esnext",
        "allowSyntheticDefaultImports": true
    }
}
```

and ts-loader will generate `import ... from ...`.

Yeah, if you have commonjs project, you can't use `import wrapAnsi from 'wrap-ansi';`, you need to use dynamic `import("wrap-ansi")`, we can't load ESM module using `require()`. There's nothing we can do with it. 

Feel free to feedback

body:
I have found the solution to my problem. Maybe this helps:
The compiled Typescript code was running on the client-side/browser. Since my webpack.config.ts file was configured to compile this code for the server side, require() (which does not exist in the browser) was used during compilation.
I changed my config.ts to compile client code files as `target: 'web',` and server code as` target: 'node'`

body:
We have an API for such purposes and other tools use it (not only webpack, other bundlers too) - https://github.com/webpack-contrib/postcss-loader/blob/master/src/index.js#L191

`@fullhuman/postcss-purgecss` should use postcss message API, i.e. run this code:
```js
result.messages.push(
  {
    type: "dependency",
    file: "/absolute/path/to/file.php",
    plugin: "@fullhuman/postcss-purgecss",
  }
)
```

Webpack knows nothing about PHP files, also just watching PHP files and rerun compilation will very ineffective, using such API (example above) webpack rebuild only modules which depend on `/absolute/path/to/file.php` (i.e. in module where you imported CSS file where class was generated), so you will have fast rebuild.

body:
I want to say it is not a bug, just change `externalsType: 'module'` to `externalsType: "import",`.

I think we should add a new value for `externalsType` - `import-module`/`module-import` (maybe the best name) and set it by default for ES modules, i.e. keep `import(...)` and `import ... from "module";` for externals how they were written
author: alexander-akait

body: Also you can specify the type for an each item using https://webpack.js.org/configuration/externals/#string
author: alexander-akait

body:
Thank you @alexander-akait , that actually solves the hoisting, but it brings that hoisting still back to the main bundle into a function. For example, `index.ts` dynamically imports `lazy.ts`, and `lazy.ts` dynamically imports an external named `external.ts`. The definition of `externals` is hoisted to the main bundle `index.js`, instead it should have been inside `external.js`

I don't think we need another `externalsType`, why would someone who specifies `module` would want the imports hoisted for dynamic imports? That feels wrong. If they want it to be hoisted, then define them as `import "foo"` instead of `import("foo")`. It feels more like a bug. By definition in webpack docs: ` webpack will output ECMAScript module syntax whenever possible`

My recommendation would be to keep `externalsType` as `module` and don't transform it, just keep it in its place.
author: mohamedmansour

body: @mohamedmansour No, it is not a bug, I can't find a discussion about it, but there was already a question about it here, there are scenarios when developers want to hoist (preload big modules in the first run), some want to load dynamically (lazy evaluation), especial in diffucult applications (you can improve perf in some cases, for example some modules can take a lot of time on import but you used them in rare cases and you can't change vendor library code), that is why I said, we need the another value to allow to keep dynamic import as dynamic and static as static. The same logic works for other values of the `externalsType` option.
author: alexander-akait
mohamedmansour

body: As I said above we just need a new value -  `import-module`, we can't keep them as is when you have `import` or `module`, because it will be breaking change and can break a lot of configurations

body:
I found that the issue has always existed since version [5.41.0](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/releases/tag/v5.41.0) which add EcmaScript modules support.

I'm willing to fix this issue, and I've spent some time looking at it.

The problem I found is that when `renderStartup` in ModuleLibraryPlugin `moduleGraph.getExportsInfo(module).exports` equals to [] which caused by `optimization.providedExports: false`.
https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/6842c9a2c9b70e760143188ffa5baa19a72799cb/lib/library/ModuleLibraryPlugin.js#L80


I have a solution that when `library === module` and  `providedExport === false` we also flag exportsInfo by `FlagDependencyExportsPlugin` but only for the entry module. Not sure if this is the optimal solution, but I tested it and it is indeed feasible. 

But I also feel that doing so violates the meaning of `providedExport`. I will send PR to fix this issue.

body:
I found something suspicious which might allow an easy fix: 

1. Various top level symbols are marked by the InnerGraphPlugin: 
https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/611bded369b5a9ea695f2669b25a6f88b67d7826/lib/optimize/InnerGraphPlugin.js#L133
2. The `InnerGraph.tagTopLevelSymbol` first defines a variable in the parser with the name and then additionally tags the variable with the top level symbol. 
https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/611bded369b5a9ea695f2669b25a6f88b67d7826/lib/optimize/InnerGraph.js#L269-L279
3. The problem: 
    1. `parser.defineVariable` [adds a variable](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/611bded369b5a9ea695f2669b25a6f88b67d7826/lib/javascript/JavascriptParser.js#L4385-L4391) with the current scope as value. 
    2. `parser.tagVariable` [also defines a variable](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/611bded369b5a9ea695f2669b25a6f88b67d7826/lib/javascript/JavascriptParser.js#L4356-L4380) but with the correct top level symbol as value. But as there is already a VariableInfo registered from `defineVariable` [resulting in the name not to be set](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/611bded369b5a9ea695f2669b25a6f88b67d7826/lib/javascript/JavascriptParser.js#L4373) 

I see two potential fixes in this code path: 

1. In `InnerGraph.tagTopLevelSymbol` we remove the call to `parser.defineVariable`. (or move it into the if-statement when a tag already exists). 
   * Feels like the safer option as the change is quite isolated and just ensures we do not double-define the variable with missing info. 
3. In `JavaScriptParser.tagVariable` we fill the name we know instead of `true` into the VariableInfo. 
   * It seems the old name was used before and things were refactored as part of https://github.com/webpack/webpack/commit/ec518945f18618d4c3b4992f129442e73dd331da I'm not so sure about the impact of this change.

Then, we check if the argument to new is an identifier (assuming it's a simple class name without further qualification) and directly check if it matches "NotWorkingClass"
This approach circumvents the need for the parser to fill freeName correctly and directly handles the class name. Try this adjustment in your code to see if it resolves the issue with NotWorkingClass expressions not triggering the hook.

I am going to attach my piece of code......
![Screenshot (44)](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/assets/138312154/0c371c39-ccf0-4613-9a43-e24a39a9b35f)


tell me if it helps you if not then we will work on this issue more

body:
Hi maintainers,

I'm K Anjith Goud, a 2nd-year Information Technology student from India. I'm exploring contributions for GSoC 2025 and I’m particularly interested in Webpack due to my strong background in JavaScript, Node.js, and web development.

While going through open issues, I found #18113 (https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/18113) very interesting. I've successfully reproduced the issue using the sample repo provided, and I'm diving into the internals of `JavascriptParser.js` to better understand the behavior.

From what I’ve observed, the root cause seems to be that `getFreeInfoFromVariable` returns `true` instead of the class name string when the class is defined in the same file. As a result, `getMemberExpressionInfo` fails to return the necessary data, which stops `callHooksForExpression` from executing the hook.

A possible fix could be updating the logic inside `getFreeInfoFromVariable` to identify local class declarations and return their name instead of `true`, or alternatively, modifying `callHooksForExpression` to handle `true` as a valid identifier in some special cases.

I’d love to hear your thoughts on whether this direction sounds correct or if you’d suggest a different approach. I’m excited to dive deeper and contribute meaningfully to the project.

Best regards,  
K ANJITH GOUD
author: anjith1

body:
Based on the [JavascriptParser.unittest.js#L115](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/test/JavascriptParser.unittest.js#L115) here, this seems to be the expected behavior. As long as the variable is declared within the current scope (excluding undeclared globals variables or imported variables), its hooks.call won't be triggered, The behavior of hooks.new is consistent with this.

So hooks.new and hooks.call are currently only used internally by Webpack. When used directly by users, they are limited to scenarios involving undeclared global variables or imported variables.

cc @alexander-akait @hai-x
author: xiaoxiaojx

body: Users have to tag the variable `tagVariable()` at preDeclarator hook (or any hook before innerGraph call `defineVariable()` in `tagTopLevelSymbol()`, yes the order matters here) if they want call hooks for the unfree/declared variable, an example is [WorkerPlugin](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/feaa9339966416effed2ebb75a8a8ac206138018/lib/dependencies/WorkerPlugin.js#L492-L503)

body:
Thank you for the quick response!

Options 1 and 2 seem like the safest immediate fixes with minimal impact, though we acknowledge they might not cover all future scenarios.

Option 3 (and its browser-compatible variant) do offer a more universal fix. It does seem like the most ideal solution to our specific problem. However, our main concern revolves around potential side-effects across other `getValueType` usages. It would be great to know more about the specific operations `removeOperations` aims to exclude from your perspective. That would help us to gauge this option's viability better. That being said, we only pass in our custom cache object in the development environment, but browser compatibility is important to us.

I want to say it is not a bug, because BannerPlugin is not for including acustom code, but yes, you can use this for it and in some cases it will work, https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/13842 is not related to your problem, we don't remove/generate `"use strict"`, it is from your source code, I recommend to use:

```
new webpack.BannerPlugin({

  raw: true,
  include: /\.js$/,
}),
```

probably original issue was closed as a stale one.. 

So basically idea of the warning/error here is to notify user about __highly likely__ issues at runtime ( if exports are reachable code ), with unused imports it is not a problem. If you didn't disable optimisations in config, webpack will remove unused exports for you.

If you want to improve your source code, in this case better to use linters (e.g. `eslint` )

body:
Also stumbled into this issue whilst upgrading a project to Webpack 5.

I've noticed it doesn't seem to mangle _all_ of the properties in the imported JSON, just the ones referenced wherever it has been imported. For example...

Source file:

```ts
import graphqlSpec from './graphqlSpec.json'; // {"data":{"schema":{"types":[...

const { types: schemaTypes } = graphqlSpec.data.schema;

schemaTypes.reduce(...
```

Production build output:

```js
const i = JSON.parse('{"p":{"w":{"gK":[...etc'); // Objects within gK are **not** mangled

const { types: w } = i.p.w;

// Reading w fails because types has been mangled to gK
w.reduce(...
```

body:
Create a Webpack configuration with the usedExports: "global" option set.
Import a CommonJS module that has a self-reference or circular dependency inside a Web Worker.
Webpack will throw the error: Self-reference dependency has unused export name: This should not happen.
Expected Behavior
Webpack should handle self-referencing or circular dependencies correctly without throwing an error, even with usedExports: "global" set.
The usedExports: "global" optimization should work without causing issues in such cases.
Potential Workaround
Since this issue might be related to the specific optimizations when usedExports is set to "global", you could try the following:

Use a different optimization setting: Instead of "global", try using "false" or "true" for usedExports, as this could bypass the issue by not triggering the optimization that causes the error.

optimization: {
  usedExports: "false", // or "true"
}

Ensure the module structure is not self-referencing: If possible, refactor the CommonJS module to avoid circular dependencies or self-referencing exports, though this might not always be feasible depending on your codebase.

Use a different bundling strategy: Depending on your setup, consider testing alternative bundlers or adjusting your worker-related configurations to avoid relying on usedExports: "global" if that is causing the issue.

Update Webpack or Patch: Since the issue is potentially related to a Webpack bug (#12646, #12648), check if there are any newer Webpack versions or patches that address this problem. If not, you might need to apply a custom fix or rollback to a version where the issue isn't present.

Additional Resources
Review the issues on Webpack's GitHub related to circular dependencies and self-referencing modules.
Check Webpack's documentation for any updates on usedExports optimization and its behavior with CommonJS and web workers.
If you're able to isolate the issue in a minimal example (as you did in your repo), it can be helpful to provide it to the Webpack team for further investigation.

body:
Hi @alexander-akait, we recently ran into an issue with `import.meta.dirname` being undefined, and I'm interested in contributing some of the changes proposed in this thread 😌 

I glanced through the relevant codepaths (`lib/config/defaults.js`, `lib/dependencies/ImportMetaPlugin.js`, `lib/NodeStuffPlugin.js` and the test setup), and I have a sketch for how to implement this.

However, before I go down that path, I want to recap my understanding of the specification 📝  As you mentioned earlier, we will need options for `node.importMetaDirname` and `node.importMetaFilename`.

**These options seem applicable for the first phase:**
  - `'none'` or `'unsupported'`: the current default in v5. Emits these properties as undefined, with a webpack comment.
  - `false` or `'node-module'`: Webpack won't touch the `import.meta.filename`/`import.meta.dirname` code, leaving the default Node.js behavior. The dirname of the **output** file when run in a Node.js environment.
    - If the browserslist targets do not support these properties, we use an `import.meta.url` shim.
    - **Question**: Should this be the future default, when `output.module` is enabled? Can be opted-in manually, or with `futureDefaults`.

**This could be in a second phase:**
  - `true` or `'resolved-in-context'`: The dirname/filename of the input file relative to the [context option](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/entry-context/#context).
    - This is symmetric with the `__filename` and `__dirname` `true` option, because I can imagine people wanting to resolve based on the input files.
    - **Question**: Should this support both CJS and ESM output?

**And a third phase, if there is a need:**
- `node-cjs`: Replace `import.meta.filename` / `import.meta.dirname` in ESM modules with `__filename` / `__dirname` equivalents when emitting CJS.
  - This would be the inverse of the `node.__filename` / `node.__dirname` `node-module` option. I have not seen much need for this at the moment, but let me know if it makes sense.

I wrote the names to be analogous to [node.__dirname](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/node/#node__filename) and [node.__filename](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/node/#node__dirname), but I personally find the `true` and `false` values confusing. So I wrote some suggestions about stringy values, but we can bikeshed that if you prefer 😌 

**How does the above sound to you? Would it be ok if I pick up the first phase of this work?** I'd like to avoid duplicated effort, so I thought it best to let you know.

--- 

Aside: While implementing `import.meta.filename` / `import.meta.dirname` for ESM would help remove the shim and thus fix the original issue in this thread, I think the fix itself could be done in a follow-up.

body:
We are developing a server architecture where you have a "core bundle" (where the actual entry point of the application is) and several "client" ones, and we want to be able to split this code so that we can deploy the application with zero, one or just a few client bundles instead of all of them.

The rationale is twofold:
1) since we can have different on-premise deployments at different clients, and we do not wish to expose client-specific business logic to someone who isn't the intended user;
2) when updating our application deployments without altering the underlying node_modules, we would like to install only the core or client bundle(s) that actually got modified instead of the entirety of the codebase reduced to a single, monolithic bundle.

author: alexander-akait

body:
> hm, okay, we can allow to setup `binary: boolean` on the module level, so you can make something modules (in your case `asset.*`) as a non-binary, I think it is a better name and cleary here

I went ahead and pushed a slightly cleaned-up version of my original workaround fix to the PR for now, but I think I like the idea of having an override for `this.binary` on a module rule a bit more. I do think that if we go that route, there should be some documentation somewhere about the fact that Asset Modules will **not** receive source maps unless the `binary: false` override is used.
author: ArcanoxDragon

body: Yeah, we just update our docs
author: alexander-akait

body:
> > I was planning on submitting a PR to sass-loader if this got accepted so that they can include a sourceMapUrl on the result.
> 
> I think better allow to setup `sourceMap: boolean | "from-options"` (we don't set any custom source maps options and respect your) and also we skip this - https://github.com/webpack-contrib/sass-loader/blob/master/src/index.js#L107, so you can flexible setup source maps options

Actually the main problem that I identified was not an issue with the `sourceMap` option itself, but rather the fact that the custom SCSS importer used by `sass-loader` does not include `sourceMapUrl` in its importer result:
https://github.com/webpack-contrib/sass-loader/blob/master/src/utils.js#L694

This causes source mappings for imported SCSS files to be completely garbled in the browser because it tries to use the actual contents of the file as the source URL. Adding `sourceMapUrl: canonicalUrl` fixes the issue, but I'm not sure if that's the correct fix as it would result in absolute URLs being returned for imported files.

body:
You can disable `optimization.providedExports` in development, webpack enabled it by default in development, the only case I encountered where enabling `providedExports` in development mode was effective is `re-exports + cycle reference`, `SideEffectsFlagPlugin` will use provided exports to skip the re-exports module, which avoid the error caused by cycle reference in re-exports module, but in actual usage scenarios, this situation is very rare. (I'm not sure whether there are other reasons for enabling providedExports in development by default)

In your provided reproduce, with `optimization.providedExports = false`, ~2s -> ~70ms for every rebuild
author: ahabhgk

body: Maybe good place to look how we can speedup it (cache? better logic? etc)
author: alexander-akait

body:
We experimented with disabling `optimization.providedExports`, and while this did make the development build faster, it also silenced errors related to incorrectly named exports. For example, changing a named export while in watch mode would normally show a build error if not all imports were updated with the new name, but after removing the `providedExports` optimization this was no longer the case. I'm not sure if this is a bug or expected behaviour.

At the moment our temporary solution has been to use a slightly updated version of the [old json-loader](https://github.com/webpack-contrib/json-loader) instead of using Webpack's built-in JSON generation, but it'd be great to remove this shim and get a fix in upstream.

I'm happy spending some more time on this writing a max depth PR and potentially looking at performance optimizations when I get a chance!

body:
Other concerns:
1) If the module object is ever handed to a function (or the other similar bailouts that webpack uses for export mangling), then we have to assume live bindings for everything, and also that export order may matter
1) When the value being exported is a `const`, `let`, or `var` declaration, rather than a `function` or `class`, assigning the value has to happen at the *end* of the module instead of being hoisted.

body:
Some note: 
1.  We register export definitions early before `evaluation` because in circular reference, another module may access this module’s namespace while this module is still evaluating.
2. We register export definitions by `getter` is to keep live binding behavior and to also avoid the DCE issue brought by `1`.
3. Ideally we can optimize the `export const` case when no circular module reference founded. Related implementations in other bundlers: https://github.com/web-infra-dev/rspack/pull/14045, https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/82760.

body:
I have looked into your problem and you want a universal environment, i.e. `target: ["node", "web"]`, unfortunately this is not possible for `umd`, you have `url('data:image/svg+xml;utf8,...)`, webpack handles this as a request and he needs it `__webpack_require__.b` to loader it:

- ignore `data:image/svg+xml` using configuration
- using `chunkLoading: false` as you written above
- using ESM, still in `experiments` for universal target

Feel free to feedback
author: alexander-akait

body:
If you are using Webpack5 + Vue2 to build an SSR framework, `vue-server-renderer` will have related problems when using Vue2 under Webpack5. The `server-bundle.js` packaged by webpack5 will have this statement below. (see [url-assets](https://webpack.js.org/guides/asset-modules/#url-assets))
```js
__webpack_require__.b = require('url').pathToFileURL(__filename)
```
but `vue-server-renderer` [wraps a layer of function](https://github.com/vuejs/vue/blob/9e88707940088cb1f4cd7dd210c9168a50dc347c/packages/server-renderer/src/bundle-renderer/create-bundle-runner.ts#L67C5-L67C20) and provides empty `__dirname` and `__filename`. [This PR](https://github.com/vuejs/vue/pull/12242) provides a solution but has not been officially merged by Vue so far.

body:
A project may have many modules that should be used as singletons, and a developer never knows about all of them (e.g., packages in node_modules and their dependencies)
I think that the solution is to try to use split chunk logic for exposed modules used in a federated project itself instead of managing them manually.
Current behaviour just breaks some projects and leads to unexpected errors, and an investigation to find modules that should be used as singletons takes tons of time.

author: ScriptedAlchemy
smelukov

body:
Share the thing they both need as a singleton. Sharing exposed modules as singletons won't work since an exposed module is a singleton, but the sub-dependencies in there are not, unless you mark them as such.

If you have a host where its own remote entry is consumed as well, then that's your actual issue. There are two module graphs on the page, and the instance in the host is a different runtime and module cache than the remote entry. In such a case, you need a single runtime chunk or can sometimes work around it by sharing and not exposing. But the reason it "works" is that the remote doesn't actually load a copy and the duplicate runtime happens to use one from the other. 

While the workaround works. If you do have host uses its own remote somehow in the tree. Then chunk loading will eventually break etc

author: smelukov

body:
The problem is that you have two runtime chunks. We cannot auto-fix that. (It should be a) single runtime chunk for the host to load its own remote. Tomorrow I will see if I can ship a slight update to how it works. I have something to handle making both single runtime and standalone runtime. Then you use the runtime plugin to switch between which remote URL to use depending on if the remote is also the host or not.

Single runtime means the remote won't work off the host. Or without the host's parent runtime.
If your host has other dynamic imports that load after its remote, you will get chunk load timeout or failed to load errors from webpack. Since the remote will overwrite the hosts chunk loading global.
author: ScriptedAlchemy

body:
@tomprince @alexander-akait 

I've noticed an issue in `webpack/lib/webworker/ImportScriptsChunkLoadingPlugin.js` where the `asyncChunkLoading` flag is set to `true`, causing unnecessary asynchronous chunk loading in worker entrypoints. This setting conflicts with scenarios like creating a custom worker for Monaco editor, where `importScripts` is used synchronously. Specifically, when `importScripts` is used to load a webpack bundle, the expected synchronous behavior is not maintained, leading to errors unless `asyncChunkLoading` is set to `false`.

I have experience in debugging and enhancing JavaScript build tools, and I'm confident that I can address this issue effectively by refining the chunk loading process to respect the synchronous nature of `importScripts`.

I would like to take this token. Can you please assign this to me?

Best,
Swayam.
author: Swayam-code

body: @Swayam-code Can you provide a real reproducible example?
author: alexander-akait
